GitHub Link¶

https://github.com/brandoncabrera707/cisd_43_sentiment_analysis_assignment

In [1]:
import requests
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup

# need this to conform with wikipedia settings
HEADERS = {
    "User-Agent": "cisd-43-sentiment-analysis/0.1 (educational project; contact: brandoncabrera707@gmail.com)",
    "Accept-Language": "en-US,en;q=0.9",
}
In [2]:
url = 'https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biostatistics'
response = requests.get(url, headers=HEADERS, timeout=30)
response.raise_for_status()
soup = BeautifulSoup(response.text, 'html.parser')
In [3]:
print(soup.prettify()) # information about page layout

      <header class="mw-body-header vector-page-titlebar no-font-mode-scale">

         <label aria-hidden="true" class="vector-dropdown-label cdx-button cdx-button--fake-button cdx-button--fake-button--enabled cdx-button--weight-quiet cdx-button--icon-only" for="vector-page-titlebar-toc-checkbox" id="vector-page-titlebar-toc-label">

          </span>

       </nav>
       <h1 class="firstHeading mw-first-heading" id="firstHeading">
        <span dir="ltr" lang="en">
         <span class="mw-page-title-main">
          Biostatistics
         </span>
        </span>
       </h1>
       <div class="vector-dropdown mw-portlet mw-portlet-lang" id="p-lang-btn">
        <input aria-haspopup="true" aria-label="Go to an article in another language. Available in 49 languages" class="vector-dropdown-checkbox mw-interlanguage-selector" data-event-name="ui.dropdown-p-lang-btn" id="p-lang-btn-checkbox" role="button" type="checkbox"/>

            <a class="interlanguage-link-target" data-language-autonym="閩南語 / Bân-lâm-gí" data-language-local-name="Minnan" data-title="Seng-bu̍t thóng-kè-ha̍k" href="https://zh-min-nan.wikipedia.org/wiki/Seng-bu%CC%8Dt_th%C3%B3ng-k%C3%A8-ha%CC%8Dk" hreflang="nan" lang="nan" title="Seng-bu̍t thóng-kè-ha̍k – Minnan">
             <span>
              閩南語 / Bân-lâm-gí
             </span>
            </a>
           </li>
           <li class="interlanguage-link interwiki-zh-yue mw-list-item">
            <a class="interlanguage-link-target" data-language-autonym="粵語" data-language-local-name="Cantonese" data-title="生物統計學" href="https://zh-yue.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E7%94%9F%E7%89%A9%E7%B5%B1%E8%A8%88%E5%AD%B8" hreflang="yue" lang="yue" title="生物統計學 – Cantonese">
             <span>
              粵語
             </span>
            </a>
           </li>
           <li class="interlanguage-link interwiki-zh mw-list-item">
            <a class="interlanguage-link-target" data-language-autonym="中文" data-language-local-name="Chinese" data-title="生物統計學" href="https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E7%94%9F%E7%89%A9%E7%B5%B1%E8%A8%88%E5%AD%B8" hreflang="zh" lang="zh" title="生物統計學 – Chinese">
             <span>
              中文
             </span>
            </a>
           </li>
          </ul>
          <div class="after-portlet after-portlet-lang">
           <span class="wb-langlinks-edit wb-langlinks-link">
            <a class="wbc-editpage" href="https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Special:EntityPage/Q214746#sitelinks-wikipedia" title="Edit interlanguage links">
             Edit links
            </a>
           </span>
          </div>
         </div>
        </div>
       </div>
      </header>

         </div>

         <div class="vector-pinned-container" id="vector-appearance-pinned-container">
          <div class="vector-appearance vector-pinnable-element" id="vector-appearance">
           <div class="vector-pinnable-header vector-appearance-pinnable-header vector-pinnable-header-pinned" data-feature-name="appearance-pinned" data-pinnable-element-id="vector-appearance" data-pinned-container-id="vector-appearance-pinned-container" data-unpinned-container-id="vector-appearance-unpinned-container">
            <div class="vector-pinnable-header-label">
             Appearance
            </div>

             move to sidebar
            </button>

       </div>
      </div>
      <div aria-labelledby="firstHeading" class="vector-body" data-mw-ve-target-container="" id="bodyContent">
       <div class="vector-body-before-content">
        <div class="mw-indicators">
        </div>
        <div class="noprint" id="siteSub">
         From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
        </div>
       </div>
       <div id="contentSub">
        <div id="mw-content-subtitle">
        </div>
       </div>
       <div class="mw-body-content" id="mw-content-text">
        <div class="mw-subjectpageheader">
        </div>
        <div class="mw-content-ltr mw-parser-output" dir="ltr" lang="en">
         <div class="shortdescription nomobile noexcerpt noprint searchaux" style="display:none">
          Application of statistical techniques to biological systems
         </div>
         <style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1320445320">
          .mw-parser-output .hatnote{font-style:italic}.mw-parser-output div.hatnote{padding-left:1.6em;margin-bottom:0.5em}.mw-parser-output .hatnote i{font-style:normal}.mw-parser-output .hatnote+link+.hatnote{margin-top:-0.5em}@media print{body.ns-0 .mw-parser-output .hatnote{display:none!important}}
         </style>
         <div class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable" role="note">
          "Biometry" redirects here. For the automated recognition of people based on intrinsic physical or behavioural traits, see
          <a href="/wiki/Biometrics" title="Biometrics">
           Biometrics
          </a>
          .
         </div>
         <link href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style">
          <div class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable" role="note">
           For the academic journal, see
           <a href="/wiki/Biostatistics_(journal)" title="Biostatistics (journal)">
            Biostatistics (journal)
           </a>
           .
          </div>
          <p>
           <b>
            Biostatistics
           </b>
           (sometimes referred to as
           <b>
            biometry
           </b>
           ) is a branch of
           <a href="/wiki/Statistics" title="Statistics">
            statistics
           </a>
           that applies statistical methods to a wide range of topics in the
           <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Biological_sciences" title="Biological sciences">
            biological sciences
           </a>
           , with a focus on
           <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Clinical_medicine" title="Clinical medicine">
            clinical medicine
           </a>
           and
           <a href="/wiki/Public_health" title="Public health">
            public health
           </a>
           applications.
           <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-1">
            <a href="#cite_note-1">
             <span class="cite-bracket">
              [
             </span>
             1
             <span class="cite-bracket">
              ]
             </span>
            </a>
           </sup>
           The field encompasses the design of experiments, the collection and analysis of experimental and observational data, and the interpretation of the results.
It is closely related to
           <a href="/wiki/Medical_statistics" title="Medical statistics">
            medical statistics
           </a>
           .
          </p>
          <meta property="mw:PageProp/toc">
           <div class="mw-heading mw-heading2">
            <h2 id="History">
             History
            </h2>
            <span class="mw-editsection">
             <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
              [
             </span>
             <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=1" title="Edit section: History">
              <span>
               edit
              </span>
             </a>
             <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
              ]
             </span>
            </span>
           </div>
           <div class="mw-heading mw-heading3">
            <h3 id="Biostatistics_and_genetics">
             Biostatistics and genetics
            </h3>
            <span class="mw-editsection">
             <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
              [
             </span>
             <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=2" title="Edit section: Biostatistics and genetics">
              <span>
               edit
              </span>
             </a>
             <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
              ]
             </span>
            </span>
           </div>
           <p>
            Biostatistical modeling forms an important part of numerous modern biological theories.
            <a href="/wiki/Genetics" title="Genetics">
             Genetics
            </a>
            studies, since its beginning, used statistical concepts to understand observed experimental results. Some genetics scientists even contributed with statistical advances with the development of methods and tools.
            <a href="/wiki/Gregor_Mendel" title="Gregor Mendel">
             Gregor Mendel
            </a>
            started the genetics studies investigating genetics segregation patterns in families of peas and used statistics to explain the collected data. In the early 1900s, after the rediscovery of Mendel's Mendelian inheritance work, there were gaps in understanding between genetics and evolutionary Darwinism.
            <a href="/wiki/Francis_Galton" title="Francis Galton">
             Francis Galton
            </a>
            tried to expand Mendel's discoveries with human data and proposed a different model with fractions of the heredity coming from each ancestral composing an infinite series. He called this the theory of "
            <a href="/wiki/Francis_Galton" title="Francis Galton">
             Law of Ancestral Heredity
            </a>
            ". His ideas were strongly disagreed by
            <a href="/wiki/William_Bateson" title="William Bateson">
             William Bateson
            </a>
            , who followed Mendel's conclusions, that genetic inheritance were exclusively from the parents, half from each of them. This led to a vigorous debate between the biometricians, who supported Galton's ideas, as
            <a href="/wiki/Raphael_Weldon" title="Raphael Weldon">
             Raphael Weldon
            </a>
            ,
            <a href="/wiki/Arthur_Dukinfield_Darbishire" title="Arthur Dukinfield Darbishire">
             Arthur Dukinfield Darbishire
            </a>
            and
            <a href="/wiki/Karl_Pearson" title="Karl Pearson">
             Karl Pearson
            </a>
            , and Mendelians, who supported Bateson's (and Mendel's) ideas, such as
            <a href="/wiki/Charles_Davenport" title="Charles Davenport">
             Charles Davenport
            </a>
            and
            <a href="/wiki/Wilhelm_Johannsen" title="Wilhelm Johannsen">
             Wilhelm Johannsen
            </a>
            . Later, biometricians could not reproduce Galton conclusions in different experiments, and Mendel's ideas prevailed. By the 1930s, models built on statistical reasoning had helped to resolve these differences and to produce the neo-Darwinian
            <a href="/wiki/Modern_synthesis_(20th_century)" title="Modern synthesis (20th century)">
             modern evolutionary synthesis
            </a>
            .
           </p>
           <p>
            Solving these differences also allowed to define the concept of population genetics and brought together genetics and evolution. The three leading figures in the establishment of
            <a href="/wiki/Population_genetics" title="Population genetics">
             population genetics
            </a>
            and this synthesis all relied on statistics and developed its use in biology.
           </p>
           <ul>
            <li>
             <a href="/wiki/Ronald_Fisher" title="Ronald Fisher">
              Ronald Fisher
             </a>
             worked alongside statistician Betty Allan developing several basic statistical methods in support of his work studying the crop experiments at
             <a href="/wiki/Rothamsted_Research" title="Rothamsted Research">
              Rothamsted Research
             </a>
             , published in Fisher's books
             <a href="/wiki/Statistical_Methods_for_Research_Workers" title="Statistical Methods for Research Workers">
              Statistical Methods for Research Workers
             </a>
             (1925) and
             <a href="/wiki/The_Genetical_Theory_of_Natural_Selection" title="The Genetical Theory of Natural Selection">
              The Genetical Theory of Natural Selection
             </a>
             (1930), as well as Allan's scientific papers.
             <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-2">
              <a href="#cite_note-2">
               <span class="cite-bracket">
                [
               </span>
               2
               <span class="cite-bracket">
                ]
               </span>
              </a>
             </sup>
             Fisher went on to give many contributions to genetics and statistics. Some of them include the
             <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/ANOVA" title="ANOVA">
              ANOVA
             </a>
             ,
             <a href="/wiki/P-value" title="P-value">
              p-value
             </a>
             concepts,
             <a href="/wiki/Ronald_Fisher" title="Ronald Fisher">
              Fisher's exact test
             </a>
             and
             <a href="/wiki/Ronald_Fisher" title="Ronald Fisher">
              Fisher's equation
             </a>
             for
             <a href="/wiki/Population_dynamics" title="Population dynamics">
              population dynamics
             </a>
             . He is credited for the sentence "Natural selection is a mechanism for generating an exceedingly high degree of improbability".
             <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-3">
              <a href="#cite_note-3">
               <span class="cite-bracket">
                [
               </span>
               3
               <span class="cite-bracket">
                ]
               </span>
              </a>
             </sup>
            </li>
            <li>
             <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Sewall_G._Wright" title="Sewall G. Wright">
              Sewall G. Wright
             </a>
             developed
             <a href="/wiki/F-statistics" title="F-statistics">
              <i>
               F
              </i>
              -statistics
             </a>
             and methods of computing them and defined
             <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Inbreeding_coefficient" title="Inbreeding coefficient">
              inbreeding coefficient
             </a>
             .
            </li>
            <li>
             <a href="/wiki/J._B._S._Haldane" title="J. B. S. Haldane">
              J. B. S. Haldane
             </a>
             's book,
             <i>
              The Causes of Evolution
             </i>
             , reestablished natural selection as the premier mechanism of evolution by explaining it in terms of the mathematical consequences of Mendelian genetics. He also developed the theory of
             <a href="/wiki/Primordial_soup" title="Primordial soup">
              primordial soup
             </a>
             .
            </li>
           </ul>
           <p>
            These and other biostatisticians,
            <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Mathematical_biology" title="Mathematical biology">
             mathematical biologists
            </a>
            , and statistically inclined geneticists helped bring together
            <a href="/wiki/Evolutionary_biology" title="Evolutionary biology">
             evolutionary biology
            </a>
            and
            <a href="/wiki/Genetics" title="Genetics">
             genetics
            </a>
            into a consistent, coherent whole that could begin to be
            <a href="/wiki/Statistics" title="Statistics">
             quantitatively
            </a>
            modeled.
           </p>
           <p>
            In parallel to this overall development, the pioneering work of
            <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/D%27Arcy_Thompson" title="D'Arcy Thompson">
             D'Arcy Thompson
            </a>
            in
            <i>
             On Growth and Form
            </i>
            also helped to add quantitative discipline to biological study.
           </p>
           <p>
            Despite the fundamental importance and frequent necessity of statistical reasoning, there may nonetheless have been a tendency among biologists to distrust or deprecate results which are not
            <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Qualitative_data" title="Qualitative data">
             qualitatively
            </a>
            apparent. One anecdote describes
            <a href="/wiki/Thomas_Hunt_Morgan" title="Thomas Hunt Morgan">
             Thomas Hunt Morgan
            </a>
            banning the
            <a href="/wiki/Friden,_Inc." title="Friden, Inc.">
             Friden calculator
            </a>
            from his department at
            <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Caltech" title="Caltech">
             Caltech
            </a>
            , saying "Well, I am like a guy who is prospecting for gold along the banks of the Sacramento River in 1849. With a little intelligence, I can reach down and pick up big nuggets of gold. And as long as I can do that, I'm not going to let any people in my department waste scarce resources in
            <a href="/wiki/Placer_mining" title="Placer mining">
             placer mining
            </a>
            ."
            <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-4">
             <a href="#cite_note-4">
              <span class="cite-bracket">
               [
              </span>
              4
              <span class="cite-bracket">
               ]
              </span>
             </a>
            </sup>
           </p>
           <div class="mw-heading mw-heading2">
            <h2 id="Research_planning">
             Research planning
            </h2>
            <span class="mw-editsection">
             <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
              [
             </span>
             <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=3" title="Edit section: Research planning">
              <span>
               edit
              </span>
             </a>
             <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
              ]
             </span>
            </span>
           </div>
           <p>
            Any research in
            <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Life_sciences" title="Life sciences">
             life sciences
            </a>
            is proposed to answer a
            <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Scientific_question" title="Scientific question">
             scientific question
            </a>
            we might have. To answer this question with a high certainty, we need
            <a href="/wiki/Accuracy_and_precision" title="Accuracy and precision">
             accurate
            </a>
            results. The correct definition of the main
            <a href="/wiki/Hypothesis" title="Hypothesis">
             hypothesis
            </a>
            and the research plan will reduce errors while taking a decision in understanding a phenomenon. The research plan might include the research question, the hypothesis to be tested, the
            <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Experimental_design" title="Experimental design">
             experimental design
            </a>
            ,
            <a href="/wiki/Data_collection" title="Data collection">
             data collection
            </a>
            methods,
            <a href="/wiki/Data_analysis" title="Data analysis">
             data analysis
            </a>
            perspectives and costs involved. It is essential to carry the study based on the three basic principles of experimental statistics:
            <a href="/wiki/Randomization" title="Randomization">
             randomization
            </a>
            ,
            <a href="/wiki/Replication_(statistics)" title="Replication (statistics)">
             replication
            </a>
            , and local control.
           </p>
           <div class="mw-heading mw-heading3">
            <h3 id="Research_question">
             Research question
            </h3>
            <span class="mw-editsection">
             <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
              [
             </span>
             <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=4" title="Edit section: Research question">
              <span>
               edit
              </span>
             </a>
             <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
              ]
             </span>
            </span>
           </div>
           <p>
            The research question will define the objective of a study. The research will be headed by the question, so it needs to be concise, at the same time it is focused on interesting and novel topics that may improve science and knowledge and that field. To define the way to ask the
            <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Scientific_question" title="Scientific question">
             scientific question
            </a>
            , an exhaustive
            <a href="/wiki/Literature_review" title="Literature review">
             literature review
            </a>
            might be necessary. So the research can be useful to add value to the
            <a href="/wiki/Scientific_community" title="Scientific community">
             scientific community
            </a>
            .
            <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-:3_5-0">
             <a href="#cite_note-:3-5">
              <span class="cite-bracket">
               [
              </span>
              5
              <span class="cite-bracket">
               ]
              </span>
             </a>
            </sup>
           </p>
           <div class="mw-heading mw-heading3">
            <h3 id="Hypothesis_definition">
             Hypothesis definition
            </h3>
            <span class="mw-editsection">
             <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
              [
             </span>
             <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=5" title="Edit section: Hypothesis definition">
              <span>
               edit
              </span>
             </a>
             <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
              ]
             </span>
            </span>
           </div>
           <p>
            Once the aim of the study is defined, the possible answers to the research question can be proposed, transforming this question into a
            <a href="/wiki/Hypothesis" title="Hypothesis">
             hypothesis
            </a>
            . The main propose is called
            <a href="/wiki/Null_hypothesis" title="Null hypothesis">
             null hypothesis
            </a>
            (H
            <sub>
             0
            </sub>
            ) and is usually based on a permanent knowledge about the topic or an obvious occurrence of the phenomena, sustained by a deep literature review. We can say it is the standard expected answer for the data under the situation in
            <a href="/wiki/Experiment" title="Experiment">
             test
            </a>
            . In general, H
            <sub>
             O
            </sub>
            assumes no association between treatments. On the other hand, the
            <a href="/wiki/Alternative_hypothesis" title="Alternative hypothesis">
             alternative hypothesis
            </a>
            is the denial of H
            <sub>
             O
            </sub>
            . It assumes some degree of association between the treatment and the outcome. Although, the hypothesis is sustained by question research and its expected and unexpected answers.
            <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-:3_5-1">
             <a href="#cite_note-:3-5">
              <span class="cite-bracket">
               [
              </span>
              5
              <span class="cite-bracket">
               ]
              </span>
             </a>
            </sup>
           </p>
           <p>
            As an example, consider groups of similar animals (mice, for example) under two different diet systems. The research question would be: what is the best diet? In this case, H
            <sub>
             0
            </sub>
            would be that there is no difference between the two diets in mice
            <a href="/wiki/Metabolism" title="Metabolism">
             metabolism
            </a>
            (H
            <sub>
             0
            </sub>
            : μ
            <sub>
             1
            </sub>
            = μ
            <sub>
             2
            </sub>
            ) and the
            <a href="/wiki/Alternative_hypothesis" title="Alternative hypothesis">
             alternative hypothesis
            </a>
            would be that the diets have different effects over animals metabolism (H
            <sub>
             1
            </sub>
            : μ
            <sub>
             1
            </sub>
            ≠ μ
            <sub>
             2
            </sub>
            ).
           </p>
           <p>
            The
            <a href="/wiki/Hypothesis" title="Hypothesis">
             hypothesis
            </a>
            is defined by the researcher, according to his/her interests in answering the main question. Besides that, the
            <a href="/wiki/Alternative_hypothesis" title="Alternative hypothesis">
             alternative hypothesis
            </a>
            can be more than one hypothesis. It can assume not only differences across observed parameters, but their degree of differences (
            <i>
             i.e.
            </i>
            higher or shorter).
           </p>
           <div class="mw-heading mw-heading3">
            <h3 id="Sampling">
             Sampling
            </h3>
            <span class="mw-editsection">
             <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
              [
             </span>
             <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=6" title="Edit section: Sampling">
              <span>
               edit
              </span>
             </a>
             <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
              ]
             </span>
            </span>
           </div>
           <p>
            Usually, a study aims to understand an effect of a phenomenon over a
            <a href="/wiki/Population_(biology)" title="Population (biology)">
             population
            </a>
            . In
            <a href="/wiki/Biology" title="Biology">
             biology
            </a>
            , a
            <a href="/wiki/Population_(biology)" title="Population (biology)">
             population
            </a>
            is defined as all the
            <a href="/wiki/Individual" title="Individual">
             individuals
            </a>
            of a given
            <a href="/wiki/Species" title="Species">
             species
            </a>
            , in a specific area at a given time. In biostatistics, this concept is extended to a variety of collections possible of study. Although, in biostatistics, a
            <a href="/wiki/Population_(biology)" title="Population (biology)">
             population
            </a>
            is not only the individuals, but the total of one specific component of their
            <a href="/wiki/Organism" title="Organism">
             organisms
            </a>
            , as the whole
            <a href="/wiki/Genome" title="Genome">
             genome
            </a>
            , or all the sperm
            <a href="/wiki/Cell_(biology)" title="Cell (biology)">
             cells
            </a>
            , for animals, or the total leaf area, for a plant, for example.
           </p>
           <p>
            It is not possible to take the
            <a href="/wiki/Measurement" title="Measurement">
             measures
            </a>
            from all the elements of a
            <a href="/wiki/Population_(biology)" title="Population (biology)">
             population
            </a>
            . Because of that, the
            <a href="/wiki/Sampling_(statistics)" title="Sampling (statistics)">
             sampling
            </a>
            process is very important for
            <a href="/wiki/Statistical_inference" title="Statistical inference">
             statistical inference
            </a>
            .
            <a href="/wiki/Sampling_(statistics)" title="Sampling (statistics)">
             Sampling
            </a>
            is defined as to randomly get a representative part of the entire population, to make posterior inferences about the population. So, the
            <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Sample_(statistics)" title="Sample (statistics)">
             sample
            </a>
            might catch the most
            <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Statistical_variability" title="Statistical variability">
             variability
            </a>
            across a population.
            <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-:2_6-0">
             <a href="#cite_note-:2-6">
              <span class="cite-bracket">
               [
              </span>
              6
              <span class="cite-bracket">
               ]
              </span>
             </a>
            </sup>
            The
            <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Sample_size" title="Sample size">
             sample size
            </a>
            is determined by several things, since the scope of the research to the resources available. In
            <a href="/wiki/Clinical_research" title="Clinical research">
             clinical research
            </a>
            , the trial type, as
            <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Inferiority" title="Inferiority">
             inferiority
            </a>
            ,
            <a href="/wiki/Equivalence_(measure_theory)" title="Equivalence (measure theory)">
             equivalence
            </a>
            , and
            <a href="/wiki/Superior_(hierarchy)" title="Superior (hierarchy)">
             superiority
            </a>
            is a key in determining sample
            <a href="/wiki/Size" title="Size">
             size
            </a>
            .
            <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-:3_5-2">
             <a href="#cite_note-:3-5">
              <span class="cite-bracket">
               [
              </span>
              5
              <span class="cite-bracket">
               ]
              </span>
             </a>
            </sup>
           </p>
           <div class="mw-heading mw-heading3">
            <h3 id="Experimental_design">
             Experimental design
            </h3>
            <span class="mw-editsection">
             <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
              [
             </span>
             <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=7" title="Edit section: Experimental design">
              <span>
               edit
              </span>
             </a>
             <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
              ]
             </span>
            </span>
           </div>
           <p>
            <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Experimental_designs" title="Experimental designs">
             Experimental designs
            </a>
            sustain those basic principles of
            <a href="/wiki/Design_of_experiments" title="Design of experiments">
             experimental statistics
            </a>
            . There are three basic experimental designs to randomly allocate
            <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Treatment_group" title="Treatment group">
             treatments
            </a>
            in all
            <a href="/wiki/Quadrat" title="Quadrat">
             plots
            </a>
            of the
            <a href="/wiki/Experiment" title="Experiment">
             experiment
            </a>
            . They are
            <a href="/wiki/Completely_randomized_design" title="Completely randomized design">
             completely randomized design
            </a>
            ,
            <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Randomized_block_design" title="Randomized block design">
             randomized block design
            </a>
            , and
            <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Factorial_designs" title="Factorial designs">
             factorial designs
            </a>
            . Treatments can be arranged in many ways inside the experiment. In
            <a href="/wiki/Agriculture" title="Agriculture">
             agriculture
            </a>
            , the correct
            <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Experimental_design" title="Experimental design">
             experimental design
            </a>
            is the root of a good study and the arrangement of
            <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Treatment_group" title="Treatment group">
             treatments
            </a>
            within the study is essential because
            <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Environment_(systems)" title="Environment (systems)">
             environment
            </a>
            largely affects the
            <a href="/wiki/Quadrat" title="Quadrat">
             plots
            </a>
            (
            <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Plants" title="Plants">
             plants
            </a>
            ,
            <a href="/wiki/Livestock" title="Livestock">
             livestock
            </a>
            ,
            <a href="/wiki/Microorganism" title="Microorganism">
             microorganisms
            </a>
            ). These main arrangements can be found in the literature under the names of "
            <a href="/wiki/Lattice_model_(physics)" title="Lattice model (physics)">
             lattices
            </a>
            ", "incomplete blocks", "
            <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Split_plot" title="Split plot">
             split plot
            </a>
            ", "augmented blocks", and many others. All of the designs might include
            <a href="/wiki/Scientific_control" title="Scientific control">
             control plots
            </a>
            , determined by the researcher, to provide an
            <a href="/wiki/Estimation_theory" title="Estimation theory">
             error estimation
            </a>
            during
            <a href="/wiki/Inference" title="Inference">
             inference
            </a>
            .
           </p>
           <p>
            In
            <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Clinical_studies" title="Clinical studies">
             clinical studies
            </a>
            , the
            <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Sample_(statistics)" title="Sample (statistics)">
             samples
            </a>
            are usually smaller than in other biological studies, and in most cases, the
            <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Environment_(systems)" title="Environment (systems)">
             environment
            </a>
            effect can be controlled or measured. It is common to use
            <a href="/wiki/Randomized_controlled_trial" title="Randomized controlled trial">
             randomized controlled clinical trials
            </a>
            , where results are usually compared with
            <a href="/wiki/Observational_study" title="Observational study">
             observational study
            </a>
            designs such as
            <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Case%E2%80%93control" title="Case–control">
             case–control
            </a>
            or
            <a href="/wiki/Cohort_(statistics)" title="Cohort (statistics)">
             cohort
            </a>
            .
            <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-7">
             <a href="#cite_note-7">
              <span class="cite-bracket">
               [
              </span>
              7
              <span class="cite-bracket">
               ]
              </span>
             </a>
            </sup>
           </p>
           <div class="mw-heading mw-heading3">
            <h3 id="Data_collection">
             Data collection
            </h3>
            <span class="mw-editsection">
             <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
              [
             </span>
             <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=8" title="Edit section: Data collection">
              <span>
               edit
              </span>
             </a>
             <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
              ]
             </span>
            </span>
           </div>
           <p>
            Data collection methods must be considered in research planning, because it highly influences the sample size and experimental design.
           </p>
           <p>
            Data collection varies according to the type of data. For
            <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Qualitative_data" title="Qualitative data">
             qualitative data
            </a>
            , collection can be done with structured questionnaires or by observation, considering presence or intensity of disease, using score criterion to categorize levels of occurrence.
            <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-8">
             <a href="#cite_note-8">
              <span class="cite-bracket">
               [
              </span>
              8
              <span class="cite-bracket">
               ]
              </span>
             </a>
            </sup>
            For
            <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Quantitative_data" title="Quantitative data">
             quantitative data
            </a>
            , collection is done by measuring numerical information using instruments.
           </p>
           <p>
            In agriculture and biology studies, yield data and its components can be obtained by
            <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Metric_measure" title="Metric measure">
             metric measures
            </a>
            . However, pest and disease injuries in plants are obtained by observation, considering score scales for levels of damage. Especially, in genetic studies, modern methods for data collection in field and laboratory should be considered, as high-throughput platforms for phenotyping and genotyping. These tools allow bigger experiments, while turn possible evaluate many plots in lower time than a human-based only method for data collection.
Finally, all data collected of interest must be stored in an organized data frame for further analysis.
           </p>
           <div class="mw-heading mw-heading2">
            <h2 id="Analysis_and_data_interpretation">
             Analysis and data interpretation
            </h2>
            <span class="mw-editsection">
             <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
              [
             </span>
             <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=9" title="Edit section: Analysis and data interpretation">
              <span>
               edit
              </span>
             </a>
             <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
              ]
             </span>
            </span>
           </div>
           <div class="mw-heading mw-heading3">
            <h3 id="Descriptive_tools">
             Descriptive tools
            </h3>
            <span class="mw-editsection">
             <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
              [
             </span>
             <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=10" title="Edit section: Descriptive tools">
              <span>
               edit
              </span>
             </a>
             <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
              ]
             </span>
            </span>
           </div>
           <link href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style">
            <div class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable" role="note">
             Main article:
             <a href="/wiki/Descriptive_statistics" title="Descriptive statistics">
              Descriptive statistics
             </a>
            </div>
            <p>
             Data can be represented through
             <a href="/wiki/Table_(information)" title="Table (information)">
              tables
             </a>
             or
             <a href="/wiki/Chart" title="Chart">
              graphical
             </a>
             representation, such as line charts, bar charts, histograms, scatter plot. Also,
             <a href="/wiki/Central_tendency" title="Central tendency">
              measures of central
             </a>
             tendency and
             <a href="/wiki/Statistical_dispersion" title="Statistical dispersion">
              variability
             </a>
             can be very useful to describe an overview of the data. Follow some examples:
            </p>
            <div class="mw-heading mw-heading4">
             <h4 id="Frequency_tables">
              Frequency tables
             </h4>
             <span class="mw-editsection">
              <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
               [
              </span>
              <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=11" title="Edit section: Frequency tables">
               <span>
                edit
               </span>
              </a>
              <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
               ]
              </span>
             </span>
            </div>
            <p>
             One type of table is the
             <a href="/wiki/Frequency" title="Frequency">
              frequency
             </a>
             table, which consists of data arranged in rows and columns, where the frequency is the number of occurrences or repetitions of data. Frequency can be:
             <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-9">
              <a href="#cite_note-9">
               <span class="cite-bracket">
                [
               </span>
               9
               <span class="cite-bracket">
                ]
               </span>
              </a>
             </sup>
            </p>
            <p>
             <b>
              Absolute
             </b>
             : represents the number of times that a determined value appear;
            </p>
            <p>
             <span class="mwe-math-element mwe-math-element-block">
              <span class="mwe-math-mathml-display mwe-math-mathml-a11y" style="display: none;">
               <math alttext="{\displaystyle N=f_{1}+f_{2}+f_{3}+...+f_{n}}" display="block" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML">
                <semantics>
                 <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
                  <mstyle displaystyle="true" scriptlevel="0">
                   <mi>
                    N
                   </mi>
                   <mo>
                    =
                   </mo>
                   <msub>
                    <mi>
                     f
                    </mi>
                    <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
                     <mn>
                      1
                     </mn>
                    </mrow>
                   </msub>
                   <mo>
                    +
                   </mo>
                   <msub>
                    <mi>
                     f
                    </mi>
                    <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
                     <mn>
                      2
                     </mn>
                    </mrow>
                   </msub>
                   <mo>
                    +
                   </mo>
                   <msub>
                    <mi>
                     f
                    </mi>
                    <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
                     <mn>
                      3
                     </mn>
                    </mrow>
                   </msub>
                   <mo>
                    +
                   </mo>
                   <mo>
                    .
                   </mo>
                   <mo>
                    .
                   </mo>
                   <mo>
                    .
                   </mo>
                   <mo>
                    +
                   </mo>
                   <msub>
                    <mi>
                     f
                    </mi>
                    <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
                     <mi>
                      n
                     </mi>
                    </mrow>
                   </msub>
                  </mstyle>
                 </mrow>
                 <annotation encoding="application/x-tex">
                  {\displaystyle N=f_{1}+f_{2}+f_{3}+...+f_{n}}
                 </annotation>
                </semantics>
               </math>
              </span>
              <img alt="{\displaystyle N=f_{1}+f_{2}+f_{3}+...+f_{n}}" aria-hidden="true" class="mwe-math-fallback-image-display mw-invert skin-invert" src="https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ebd1c3755ad6b5c79b530736195d388921c8dab0" style="vertical-align: -0.671ex; width:26.499ex; height:2.509ex;"/>
             </span>
            </p>
            <p>
             <b>
              Relative
             </b>
             : obtained by the division of the absolute frequency by the total number;
            </p>
            <p>
             <span class="mwe-math-element mwe-math-element-block">
              <span class="mwe-math-mathml-display mwe-math-mathml-a11y" style="display: none;">
               <math alttext="{\displaystyle n_{i}={\frac {f_{i}}{N}}}" display="block" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML">
                <semantics>
                 <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
                  <mstyle displaystyle="true" scriptlevel="0">
                   <msub>
                    <mi>
                     n
                    </mi>
                    <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
                     <mi>
                      i
                     </mi>
                    </mrow>
                   </msub>
                   <mo>
                    =
                   </mo>
                   <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
                    <mfrac>
                     <msub>
                      <mi>
                       f
                      </mi>
                      <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
                       <mi>
                        i
                       </mi>
                      </mrow>
                     </msub>
                     <mi>
                      N
                     </mi>
                    </mfrac>
                   </mrow>
                  </mstyle>
                 </mrow>
                 <annotation encoding="application/x-tex">
                  {\displaystyle n_{i}={\frac {f_{i}}{N}}}
                 </annotation>
                </semantics>
               </math>
              </span>
              <img alt="{\displaystyle n_{i}={\frac {f_{i}}{N}}}" aria-hidden="true" class="mwe-math-fallback-image-display mw-invert skin-invert" src="https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/774addbcf9a5a2a448a6218567b78d523731571a" style="vertical-align: -1.838ex; width:8.193ex; height:5.343ex;"/>
             </span>
            </p>
            <p>
             In the next example, we have the number of genes in ten
             <a href="/wiki/Operon" title="Operon">
              operons
             </a>
             of the same organism.
            </p>
            <dl>
             <dd>
              <span class="texhtml">
               Genes = {2,3,3,4,5,3,3,3,3,4}
              </span>
             </dd>
            </dl>
            <table class="wikitable">
             <caption>
             </caption>
             <tbody>
              <tr>
               <th>
                Genes number
               </th>
               <th>
                Absolute frequency
               </th>
               <th>
                Relative frequency
               </th>
              </tr>
              <tr>
               <td>
                1
               </td>
               <td>
                0
               </td>
               <td>
                0
               </td>
              </tr>
              <tr>
               <td>
                2
               </td>
               <td>
                1
               </td>
               <td>
                0.1
               </td>
              </tr>
              <tr>
               <td>
                3
               </td>
               <td>
                6
               </td>
               <td>
                0.6
               </td>
              </tr>
              <tr>
               <td>
                4
               </td>
               <td>
                2
               </td>
               <td>
                0.2
               </td>
              </tr>
              <tr>
               <td>
                5
               </td>
               <td>
                1
               </td>
               <td>
                0.1
               </td>
              </tr>
             </tbody>
            </table>
            <div class="mw-heading mw-heading4">
             <h4 id="Line_graph">
              Line graph
             </h4>
             <span class="mw-editsection">
              <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
               [
              </span>
              <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=12" title="Edit section: Line graph">
               <span>
                edit
               </span>
              </a>
              <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
               ]
              </span>
             </span>
            </div>
            <figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb">
             <a class="mw-file-description" href="/wiki/File:Examples_of_descriptive_tools.png">
              <img class="mw-file-element" data-file-height="1138" data-file-width="1403" decoding="async" height="203" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Examples_of_descriptive_tools.png/250px-Examples_of_descriptive_tools.png" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Examples_of_descriptive_tools.png/500px-Examples_of_descriptive_tools.png 2x" width="250"/>
             </a>
             <figcaption>
              Figure A:
              <b>
               Line graph example
              </b>
              . The birth rate in Brazil (2010–2016);
              <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-:1_10-0">
               <a href="#cite_note-:1-10">
                <span class="cite-bracket">
                 [
                </span>
                10
                <span class="cite-bracket">
                 ]
                </span>
               </a>
              </sup>
              Figure B:
              <b>
               Bar chart example.
              </b>
              The birth rate in
              <a href="/wiki/Brazil" title="Brazil">
               Brazil
              </a>
              for the December months from 2010 to 2016; Figure C:
              <b>
               Example of Box Plot
              </b>
              : number of glycines in the proteome of eight different organisms (A-H); Figure D:
              <b>
               Example of a scatter plot.
              </b>
             </figcaption>
            </figure>
            <p>
             <a href="/wiki/Line_graph" title="Line graph">
              Line graphs
             </a>
             represent the variation of a value over another metric, such as time. In general, values are represented in the vertical axis, while the time variation is represented in the horizontal axis.
             <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-:0_11-0">
              <a href="#cite_note-:0-11">
               <span class="cite-bracket">
                [
               </span>
               11
               <span class="cite-bracket">
                ]
               </span>
              </a>
             </sup>
            </p>
            <div class="mw-heading mw-heading4">
             <h4 id="Bar_chart">
              Bar chart
             </h4>
             <span class="mw-editsection">
              <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
               [
              </span>
              <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=13" title="Edit section: Bar chart">
               <span>
                edit
               </span>
              </a>
              <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
               ]
              </span>
             </span>
            </div>
            <p>
             A
             <a href="/wiki/Bar_chart" title="Bar chart">
              bar chart
             </a>
             is a graph that shows categorical data as bars presenting heights (vertical bar) or widths (horizontal bar) proportional to represent values. Bar charts provide an image that could also be represented in a tabular format.
             <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-:0_11-1">
              <a href="#cite_note-:0-11">
               <span class="cite-bracket">
                [
               </span>
               11
               <span class="cite-bracket">
                ]
               </span>
              </a>
             </sup>
            </p>
            <p>
             In the bar chart example, we have the birth rate in Brazil for the December months from 2010 to 2016.
             <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-:1_10-1">
              <a href="#cite_note-:1-10">
               <span class="cite-bracket">
                [
               </span>
               10
               <span class="cite-bracket">
                ]
               </span>
              </a>
             </sup>
             The sharp fall in December 2016 reflects the outbreak of
             <a href="/wiki/Zika_virus" title="Zika virus">
              Zika virus
             </a>
             in the birth rate in Brazil.
            </p>
            <div class="mw-heading mw-heading4">
             <h4 id="Histograms">
              Histograms
             </h4>
             <span class="mw-editsection">
              <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
               [
              </span>
              <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=14" title="Edit section: Histograms">
               <span>
                edit
               </span>
              </a>
              <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
               ]
              </span>
             </span>
            </div>
            <figure typeof="mw:File/Thumb">
             <a class="mw-file-description" href="/wiki/File:Example_histogram.png">
              <img class="mw-file-element" data-file-height="350" data-file-width="300" decoding="async" height="350" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/1/1d/Example_histogram.png" width="300"/>
             </a>
             <figcaption>
              <b>
               Example of a histogram.
              </b>
             </figcaption>
            </figure>
            <p>
             The
             <a href="/wiki/Histogram" title="Histogram">
              histogram
             </a>
             (or frequency distribution) is a graphical representation of a dataset tabulated and divided into uniform or non-uniform classes. It was first introduced by
             <a href="/wiki/Karl_Pearson" title="Karl Pearson">
              Karl Pearson
             </a>
             .
             <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-12">
              <a href="#cite_note-12">
               <span class="cite-bracket">
                [
               </span>
               12
               <span class="cite-bracket">
                ]
               </span>
              </a>
             </sup>
            </p>
            <div class="mw-heading mw-heading4">
             <h4 id="Scatter_plot">
              Scatter plot
             </h4>
             <span class="mw-editsection">
              <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
               [
              </span>
              <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=15" title="Edit section: Scatter plot">
               <span>
                edit
               </span>
              </a>
              <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
               ]
              </span>
             </span>
            </div>
            <p>
             A
             <a href="/wiki/Scatter_plot" title="Scatter plot">
              scatter plot
             </a>
             is a mathematical diagram that uses Cartesian coordinates to display values of a dataset. A scatter plot shows the data as a set of points, each one presenting the value of one variable determining the position on the horizontal axis and another variable on the vertical axis.
             <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-13">
              <a href="#cite_note-13">
               <span class="cite-bracket">
                [
               </span>
               13
               <span class="cite-bracket">
                ]
               </span>
              </a>
             </sup>
             They are also called
             <b>
              scatter graph
             </b>
             ,
             <b>
              scatter chart
             </b>
             ,
             <b>
              scattergram
             </b>
             , or
             <b>
              scatter diagram
             </b>
             .
             <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-14">
              <a href="#cite_note-14">
               <span class="cite-bracket">
                [
               </span>
               14
               <span class="cite-bracket">
                ]
               </span>
              </a>
             </sup>
            </p>
            <div class="mw-heading mw-heading4">
             <h4 id="Mean">
              Mean
             </h4>
             <span class="mw-editsection">
              <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
               [
              </span>
              <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=16" title="Edit section: Mean">
               <span>
                edit
               </span>
              </a>
              <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
               ]
              </span>
             </span>
            </div>
            <link href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style">
             <div class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable" role="note">
              Main article:
              <a href="/wiki/Mean" title="Mean">
               Mean
              </a>
             </div>
             <p>
              The
              <a href="/wiki/Arithmetic_mean" title="Arithmetic mean">
               arithmetic mean
              </a>
              is the sum of a collection of values (
              <span class="mwe-math-element mwe-math-element-inline">
               <span class="mwe-math-mathml-inline mwe-math-mathml-a11y" style="display: none;">
                <math alttext="{\displaystyle {x_{1}+x_{2}+x_{3}+\cdots +x_{n}}}" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML">
                 <semantics>
                  <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
                   <mstyle displaystyle="true" scriptlevel="0">
                    <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
                     <msub>
                      <mi>
                       x
                      </mi>
                      <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
                       <mn>
                        1
                       </mn>
                      </mrow>
                     </msub>
                     <mo>
                      +
                     </mo>
                     <msub>
                      <mi>
                       x
                      </mi>
                      <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
                       <mn>
                        2
                       </mn>
                      </mrow>
                     </msub>
                     <mo>
                      +
                     </mo>
                     <msub>
                      <mi>
                       x
                      </mi>
                      <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
                       <mn>
                        3
                       </mn>
                      </mrow>
                     </msub>
                     <mo>
                      +
                     </mo>
                     <mo>
                      ⋯
                      <!-- ⋯ -->
                     </mo>
                     <mo>
                      +
                     </mo>
                     <msub>
                      <mi>
                       x
                      </mi>
                      <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
                       <mi>
                        n
                       </mi>
                      </mrow>
                     </msub>
                    </mrow>
                   </mstyle>
                  </mrow>
                  <annotation encoding="application/x-tex">
                   {\displaystyle {x_{1}+x_{2}+x_{3}+\cdots +x_{n}}}
                  </annotation>
                 </semantics>
                </math>
               </span>
               <img alt="{\displaystyle {x_{1}+x_{2}+x_{3}+\cdots +x_{n}}}" aria-hidden="true" class="mwe-math-fallback-image-inline mw-invert skin-invert" src="https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e6c1de186e51cb040c321726c133864c7e37dca1" style="vertical-align: -0.671ex; width:23.785ex; height:2.343ex;"/>
              </span>
              ) divided by the number of items of this collection (
              <span class="mwe-math-element mwe-math-element-inline">
               <span class="mwe-math-mathml-inline mwe-math-mathml-a11y" style="display: none;">
                <math alttext="{\displaystyle {n}}" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML">
                 <semantics>
                  <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
                   <mstyle displaystyle="true" scriptlevel="0">
                    <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
                     <mi>
                      n
                     </mi>
                    </mrow>
                   </mstyle>
                  </mrow>
                  <annotation encoding="application/x-tex">
                   {\displaystyle {n}}
                  </annotation>
                 </semantics>
                </math>
               </span>
               <img alt="{\displaystyle {n}}" aria-hidden="true" class="mwe-math-fallback-image-inline mw-invert skin-invert" src="https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/70b6881107a598c20a60e72fe82bc41a4a1f7f4c" style="vertical-align: -0.338ex; width:1.395ex; height:1.676ex;"/>
              </span>
              ).
             </p>
             <dl>
              <dd>
               <span class="mwe-math-element mwe-math-element-inline">
                <span class="mwe-math-mathml-inline mwe-math-mathml-a11y" style="display: none;">
                 <math alttext="{\displaystyle {\bar {x}}={\frac {1}{n}}\left(\sum _{i=1}^{n}{x_{i}}\right)={\frac {x_{1}+x_{2}+\cdots +x_{n}}{n}}}" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML">
                  <semantics>
                   <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
                    <mstyle displaystyle="true" scriptlevel="0">
                     <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
                      <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
                       <mover>
                        <mi>
                         x
                        </mi>
                        <mo stretchy="false">
                         ¯
                         <!-- ¯ -->
                        </mo>
                       </mover>
                      </mrow>
                     </mrow>
                     <mo>
                      =
                     </mo>
                     <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
                      <mfrac>
                       <mn>
                        1
                       </mn>
                       <mi>
                        n
                       </mi>
                      </mfrac>
                     </mrow>
                     <mrow>
                      <mo>
                       (
                      </mo>
                      <mrow>
                       <munderover>
                        <mo>
                         ∑
                         <!-- ∑ -->
                        </mo>
                        <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
                         <mi>
                          i
                         </mi>
                         <mo>
                          =
                         </mo>
                         <mn>
                          1
                         </mn>
                        </mrow>
                        <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
                         <mi>
                          n
                         </mi>
                        </mrow>
                       </munderover>
                       <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
                        <msub>
                         <mi>
                          x
                         </mi>
                         <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
                          <mi>
                           i
                          </mi>
                         </mrow>
                        </msub>
                       </mrow>
                      </mrow>
                      <mo>
                       )
                      </mo>
                     </mrow>
                     <mo>
                      =
                     </mo>
                     <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
                      <mfrac>
                       <mrow>
                        <msub>
                         <mi>
                          x
                         </mi>
                         <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
                          <mn>
                           1
                          </mn>
                         </mrow>
                        </msub>
                        <mo>
                         +
                        </mo>
                        <msub>
                         <mi>
                          x
                         </mi>
                         <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
                          <mn>
                           2
                          </mn>
                         </mrow>
                        </msub>
                        <mo>
                         +
                        </mo>
                        <mo>
                         ⋯
                         <!-- ⋯ -->
                        </mo>
                        <mo>
                         +
                        </mo>
                        <msub>
                         <mi>
                          x
                         </mi>
                         <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
                          <mi>
                           n
                          </mi>
                         </mrow>
                        </msub>
                       </mrow>
                       <mi>
                        n
                       </mi>
                      </mfrac>
                     </mrow>
                    </mstyle>
                   </mrow>
                   <annotation encoding="application/x-tex">
                    {\displaystyle {\bar {x}}={\frac {1}{n}}\left(\sum _{i=1}^{n}{x_{i}}\right)={\frac {x_{1}+x_{2}+\cdots +x_{n}}{n}}}
                   </annotation>
                  </semantics>
                 </math>
                </span>
                <img alt="{\displaystyle {\bar {x}}={\frac {1}{n}}\left(\sum _{i=1}^{n}{x_{i}}\right)={\frac {x_{1}+x_{2}+\cdots +x_{n}}{n}}}" aria-hidden="true" class="mwe-math-fallback-image-inline mw-invert skin-invert" src="https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4e3313161244f8ab61d897fb6e5fbf6647e1d5f5" style="vertical-align: -3.171ex; width:39.094ex; height:7.509ex;"/>
               </span>
              </dd>
             </dl>
             <div class="mw-heading mw-heading4">
              <h4 id="Median">
               Median
              </h4>
              <span class="mw-editsection">
               <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                [
               </span>
               <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=17" title="Edit section: Median">
                <span>
                 edit
                </span>
               </a>
               <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                ]
               </span>
              </span>
             </div>
             <link href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style">
              <div class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable" role="note">
               Main article:
               <a href="/wiki/Median" title="Median">
                Median
               </a>
              </div>
              <p>
               The
               <a href="/wiki/Median" title="Median">
                median
               </a>
               is the value in the middle of a dataset.
              </p>
              <div class="mw-heading mw-heading4">
               <h4 id="Mode">
                Mode
               </h4>
               <span class="mw-editsection">
                <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                 [
                </span>
                <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=18" title="Edit section: Mode">
                 <span>
                  edit
                 </span>
                </a>
                <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                 ]
                </span>
               </span>
              </div>
              <link href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style">
               <div class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable" role="note">
                Main article:
                <a href="/wiki/Mode_(statistics)" title="Mode (statistics)">
                 Mode (statistics)
                </a>
               </div>
               <p>
                The
                <a href="/wiki/Mode_(statistics)" title="Mode (statistics)">
                 mode
                </a>
                is the value of a set of data that appears most often.
                <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-15">
                 <a href="#cite_note-15">
                  <span class="cite-bracket">
                   [
                  </span>
                  15
                  <span class="cite-bracket">
                   ]
                  </span>
                 </a>
                </sup>
               </p>
               <table class="wikitable">
                <caption>
                 Comparison among mean, median and mode
                 <br/>
                 Values = { 2,3,3,3,3,3,4,4,11 }
                </caption>
                <tbody>
                 <tr>
                  <th>
                   Type
                  </th>
                  <th>
                   Example
                  </th>
                  <th>
                   Result
                  </th>
                 </tr>
                 <tr>
                  <td align="center">
                   <a href="/wiki/Arithmetic_mean" title="Arithmetic mean">
                    Mean
                   </a>
                  </td>
                  <td align="center">
                   ( 2 + 3 + 3 + 3 + 3 + 3 + 4 + 4 + 11 ) / 9
                  </td>
                  <td align="center">
                   <b>
                    4
                   </b>
                  </td>
                 </tr>
                 <tr>
                  <td align="center">
                   <a href="/wiki/Median" title="Median">
                    Median
                   </a>
                  </td>
                  <td align="center">
                   2, 3, 3, 3,
                   <b>
                    3
                   </b>
                   , 3, 4, 4, 11
                  </td>
                  <td align="center">
                   <b>
                    3
                   </b>
                  </td>
                 </tr>
                 <tr>
                  <td align="center">
                   Mode
                  </td>
                  <td align="center">
                   2,
                   <b>
                    3, 3, 3, 3, 3
                   </b>
                   , 4, 4, 11
                  </td>
                  <td align="center">
                   <b>
                    3
                   </b>
                  </td>
                 </tr>
                </tbody>
               </table>
               <div class="mw-heading mw-heading4">
                <h4 id="Box_plot">
                 Box plot
                </h4>
                <span class="mw-editsection">
                 <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                  [
                 </span>
                 <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=19" title="Edit section: Box plot">
                  <span>
                   edit
                  </span>
                 </a>
                 <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                  ]
                 </span>
                </span>
               </div>
               <p>
                <a href="/wiki/Box_plot" title="Box plot">
                 Box plot
                </a>
                is a method for graphically depicting groups of numerical data. The maximum and minimum values are represented by the lines, and the interquartile range (IQR) represent 25–75% of the data.
                <a href="/wiki/Outlier" title="Outlier">
                 Outliers
                </a>
                may be plotted as circles.
               </p>
               <div class="mw-heading mw-heading4">
                <h4 id="Correlation_coefficients">
                 Correlation coefficients
                </h4>
                <span class="mw-editsection">
                 <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                  [
                 </span>
                 <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=20" title="Edit section: Correlation coefficients">
                  <span>
                   edit
                  </span>
                 </a>
                 <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                  ]
                 </span>
                </span>
               </div>
               <p>
                Although correlations between two different kinds of data could be inferred by graphs, such as scatter plot, it is necessary validate this though numerical information. For this reason,
                <a href="/wiki/Correlation_coefficient" title="Correlation coefficient">
                 correlation coefficients
                </a>
                are required. They provide a numerical value that reflects the strength of an association.
                <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-:0_11-2">
                 <a href="#cite_note-:0-11">
                  <span class="cite-bracket">
                   [
                  </span>
                  11
                  <span class="cite-bracket">
                   ]
                  </span>
                 </a>
                </sup>
               </p>
               <div class="mw-heading mw-heading4">
                <h4 id="Pearson_correlation_coefficient">
                 Pearson correlation coefficient
                </h4>
                <span class="mw-editsection">
                 <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                  [
                 </span>
                 <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=21" title="Edit section: Pearson correlation coefficient">
                  <span>
                   edit
                  </span>
                 </a>
                 <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                  ]
                 </span>
                </span>
               </div>
               <figure class="mw-default-size mw-halign-right" typeof="mw:File/Thumb">
                <a class="mw-file-description" href="/wiki/File:Correlation_coefficient.png">
                 <img class="mw-file-element" data-file-height="530" data-file-width="960" decoding="async" height="138" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Correlation_coefficient.png/250px-Correlation_coefficient.png" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Correlation_coefficient.png/500px-Correlation_coefficient.png 2x" width="250"/>
                </a>
                <figcaption>
                 Scatter diagram that demonstrates the Pearson correlation for different values of
                 <i>
                  ρ.
                 </i>
                </figcaption>
               </figure>
               <p>
                <a href="/wiki/Pearson_correlation_coefficient" title="Pearson correlation coefficient">
                 Pearson correlation coefficient
                </a>
                is a measure of association between two variables, X and Y. This coefficient, usually represented by
                <i>
                 ρ
                </i>
                (rho) for the population and
                <i>
                 r
                </i>
                for the sample, assumes values between −1 and 1, where
                <i>
                 ρ
                </i>
                = 1 represents a perfect positive correlation,
                <i>
                 ρ
                </i>
                = −1 represents a perfect negative correlation, and
                <i>
                 ρ
                </i>
                = 0 is no linear correlation.
                <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-:0_11-3">
                 <a href="#cite_note-:0-11">
                  <span class="cite-bracket">
                   [
                  </span>
                  11
                  <span class="cite-bracket">
                   ]
                  </span>
                 </a>
                </sup>
               </p>
               <div class="mw-heading mw-heading3">
                <h3 id="Inferential_statistics">
