                 Inferential statistics
                </h3>
                <span class="mw-editsection">
                 <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                  [
                 </span>
                 <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=22" title="Edit section: Inferential statistics">
                  <span>
                   edit
                  </span>
                 </a>
                 <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                  ]
                 </span>
                </span>
               </div>
               <link href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style">
                <div class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable" role="note">
                 Main article:
                 <a href="/wiki/Statistical_inference" title="Statistical inference">
                  Statistical inference
                 </a>
                </div>
                <p>
                 It is used to make
                 <a href="/wiki/Inference" title="Inference">
                  inferences
                 </a>
                 <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-16">
                  <a href="#cite_note-16">
                   <span class="cite-bracket">
                    [
                   </span>
                   16
                   <span class="cite-bracket">
                    ]
                   </span>
                  </a>
                 </sup>
                 about an unknown population, by estimation and/or hypothesis testing. In other words, it is desirable to obtain parameters to describe the population of interest, but since the data is limited, it is necessary to make use of a representative sample in order to estimate them. With that, it is possible to test previously defined hypotheses and apply the conclusions to the entire population. The
                 <a href="/wiki/Standard_error" title="Standard error">
                  standard error of the mean
                 </a>
                 is a measure of variability that is crucial to do inferences.
                 <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-:2_6-1">
                  <a href="#cite_note-:2-6">
                   <span class="cite-bracket">
                    [
                   </span>
                   6
                   <span class="cite-bracket">
                    ]
                   </span>
                  </a>
                 </sup>
                </p>
                <ul>
                 <li>
                  <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Statistical_hypothesis_testing" title="Statistical hypothesis testing">
                   Hypothesis testing
                  </a>
                 </li>
                </ul>
                <p>
                 Hypothesis testing is essential to make inferences about populations aiming to answer research questions, as settled in "Research planning" section. Authors defined four steps to be set:
                 <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-:2_6-2">
                  <a href="#cite_note-:2-6">
                   <span class="cite-bracket">
                    [
                   </span>
                   6
                   <span class="cite-bracket">
                    ]
                   </span>
                  </a>
                 </sup>
                </p>
                <ol>
                 <li>
                  <i>
                   The hypothesis to be tested
                  </i>
                  : as stated earlier, we have to work with the definition of a
                  <a href="/wiki/Null_hypothesis" title="Null hypothesis">
                   null hypothesis
                  </a>
                  (H
                  <sub>
                   0
                  </sub>
                  ), that is going to be tested, and an
                  <a href="/wiki/Alternative_hypothesis" title="Alternative hypothesis">
                   alternative hypothesis
                  </a>
                  . But they must be defined before the experiment implementation.
                 </li>
                 <li>
                  <i>
                   Significance level and decision rule
                  </i>
                  : A decision rule depends on the
                  <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Significance_level" title="Significance level">
                   level of significance
                  </a>
                  , or in other words, the acceptable error rate (α). It is easier to think that we define a
                  <i>
                   critical value
                  </i>
                  that determines the statistical significance when a
                  <a href="/wiki/Test_statistic" title="Test statistic">
                   test statistic
                  </a>
                  is compared with it. So, α also has to be predefined before the experiment.
                 </li>
                 <li>
                  <i>
                   Experiment and statistical analysis
                  </i>
                  : This is when the experiment is really implemented following the appropriate
                  <a href="/wiki/Design_of_experiments" title="Design of experiments">
                   experimental design
                  </a>
                  , data is collected and the more suitable statistical tests are evaluated.
                 </li>
                 <li>
                  <i>
                   Inference
                  </i>
                  : Is made when the
                  <a href="/wiki/Null_hypothesis" title="Null hypothesis">
                   null hypothesis
                  </a>
                  is rejected or not rejected, based on the evidence that the comparison of
                  <a href="/wiki/P-value" title="P-value">
                   p-values
                  </a>
                  and α brings. It is pointed that the failure to reject H
                  <sub>
                   0
                  </sub>
                  just means that there is not enough evidence to support its rejection, but not that this hypothesis is true.
                 </li>
                </ol>
                <ul>
                 <li>
                  <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Confidence_intervals" title="Confidence intervals">
                   Confidence intervals
                  </a>
                 </li>
                </ul>
                <p>
                 A confidence interval is a range of values that can contain the true real parameter value in given a certain level of confidence. The first step is to estimate the best-unbiased estimate of the population parameter. The upper value of the interval is obtained by the sum of this estimate with the multiplication between the standard error of the mean and the confidence level. The calculation of lower value is similar, but instead of a sum, a subtraction must be applied.
                 <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-:2_6-3">
                  <a href="#cite_note-:2-6">
                   <span class="cite-bracket">
                    [
                   </span>
                   6
                   <span class="cite-bracket">
                    ]
                   </span>
                  </a>
                 </sup>
                </p>
                <div class="mw-heading mw-heading2">
                 <h2 id="Statistical_considerations">
                  Statistical considerations
                 </h2>
                 <span class="mw-editsection">
                  <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                   [
                  </span>
                  <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=23" title="Edit section: Statistical considerations">
                   <span>
                    edit
                   </span>
                  </a>
                  <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                   ]
                  </span>
                 </span>
                </div>
                <div class="mw-heading mw-heading3">
                 <h3 id="Power_and_statistical_error">
                  Power and statistical error
                 </h3>
                 <span class="mw-editsection">
                  <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                   [
                  </span>
                  <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=24" title="Edit section: Power and statistical error">
                   <span>
                    edit
                   </span>
                  </a>
                  <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                   ]
                  </span>
                 </span>
                </div>
                <p>
                 When testing a hypothesis, there are two types of statistic errors possible:
                 <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Type_I_error" title="Type I error">
                  Type I error
                 </a>
                 and
                 <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Type_II_error" title="Type II error">
                  Type II error
                 </a>
                 .
                </p>
                <ul>
                 <li>
                  The type I error or
                  <a href="/wiki/False_positives_and_false_negatives" title="False positives and false negatives">
                   false positive
                  </a>
                  is the incorrect rejection of a true null hypothesis
                 </li>
                 <li>
                  The type II error or
                  <a href="/wiki/False_positives_and_false_negatives" title="False positives and false negatives">
                   false negative
                  </a>
                  is the failure to reject a false
                  <a href="/wiki/Null_hypothesis" title="Null hypothesis">
                   null hypothesis
                  </a>
                  .
                 </li>
                </ul>
                <p>
                 The
                 <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Significance_level" title="Significance level">
                  significance level
                 </a>
                 denoted by α is the type I error rate and should be chosen before performing the test. The type II error rate is denoted by β and
                 <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Statistical_power" title="Statistical power">
                  statistical power of the test
                 </a>
                 is 1 − β.
                </p>
                <div class="mw-heading mw-heading3">
                 <h3 id="p-value">
                  p-value
                 </h3>
                 <span class="mw-editsection">
                  <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                   [
                  </span>
                  <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=25" title="Edit section: p-value">
                   <span>
                    edit
                   </span>
                  </a>
                  <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                   ]
                  </span>
                 </span>
                </div>
                <p>
                 The
                 <a href="/wiki/P-value" title="P-value">
                  p-value
                 </a>
                 is the probability of obtaining results as extreme as or more extreme than those observed, assuming the
                 <a href="/wiki/Null_hypothesis" title="Null hypothesis">
                  null hypothesis
                 </a>
                 (H
                 <sub>
                  0
                 </sub>
                 ) is true. It is also called the calculated probability. It is common to confuse the p-value with the
                 <a href="/wiki/Statistical_significance" title="Statistical significance">
                  significance level (α)
                 </a>
                 , but, the α is a predefined threshold for calling significant results. If p is less than α, the null hypothesis (H
                 <sub>
                  0
                 </sub>
                 ) is rejected.
                 <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-17">
                  <a href="#cite_note-17">
                   <span class="cite-bracket">
                    [
                   </span>
                   17
                   <span class="cite-bracket">
                    ]
                   </span>
                  </a>
                 </sup>
                </p>
                <div class="mw-heading mw-heading3">
                 <h3 id="Multiple_testing">
                  Multiple testing
                 </h3>
                 <span class="mw-editsection">
                  <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                   [
                  </span>
                  <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=26" title="Edit section: Multiple testing">
                   <span>
                    edit
                   </span>
                  </a>
                  <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                   ]
                  </span>
                 </span>
                </div>
                <p>
                 In multiple tests of the same hypothesis, the probability of the occurrence of
                 <a href="/wiki/False_positives_and_false_negatives" title="False positives and false negatives">
                  false positives
                 </a>
                 <a href="/wiki/Family-wise_error_rate" title="Family-wise error rate">
                  (familywise error rate)
                 </a>
                 increase and a strategy is needed to account for this occurrence. This is commonly achieved by using a more stringent threshold to reject null hypotheses. The
                 <a href="/wiki/Bonferroni_correction" title="Bonferroni correction">
                  Bonferroni correction
                 </a>
                 defines an acceptable global significance level, denoted by α* and each test is individually compared with a value of α =  α*/m. This ensures that the familywise error rate in all m tests, is less than or equal to α*. When m is large, the Bonferroni correction may be overly conservative. An alternative to the Bonferroni correction is to control the
                 <a href="/wiki/False_discovery_rate" title="False discovery rate">
                  false discovery rate (FDR)
                 </a>
                 . The FDR controls the expected proportion of the rejected
                 <a href="/wiki/Null_hypothesis" title="Null hypothesis">
                  null hypotheses
                 </a>
                 (the so-called discoveries) that are false (incorrect rejections). This procedure ensures that, for independent tests, the false discovery rate is at most q*. Thus, the FDR is less conservative than the Bonferroni correction and have more power, at the cost of more false positives.
                 <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-18">
                  <a href="#cite_note-18">
                   <span class="cite-bracket">
                    [
                   </span>
                   18
                   <span class="cite-bracket">
                    ]
                   </span>
                  </a>
                 </sup>
                </p>
                <div class="mw-heading mw-heading3">
                 <h3 id="Mis-specification_and_robustness_checks">
                  Mis-specification and robustness checks
                 </h3>
                 <span class="mw-editsection">
                  <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                   [
                  </span>
                  <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=27" title="Edit section: Mis-specification and robustness checks">
                   <span>
                    edit
                   </span>
                  </a>
                  <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                   ]
                  </span>
                 </span>
                </div>
                <p>
                 The main hypothesis being tested (e.g., no association between treatments and outcomes) is often accompanied by other technical assumptions (e.g., about the form of the probability distribution of the outcomes) that are also part of the null hypothesis. When the technical assumptions are violated in practice, then the null may be frequently rejected even if the main hypothesis is true. Such rejections are said to be due to model mis-specification.
                 <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-19">
                  <a href="#cite_note-19">
                   <span class="cite-bracket">
                    [
                   </span>
                   19
                   <span class="cite-bracket">
                    ]
                   </span>
                  </a>
                 </sup>
                 Verifying whether the outcome of a statistical test does not change when the technical assumptions are slightly altered (so-called robustness checks) is the main way of combating mis-specification.
                </p>
                <div class="mw-heading mw-heading3">
                 <h3 id="Model_selection_criteria">
                  Model selection criteria
                 </h3>
                 <span class="mw-editsection">
                  <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                   [
                  </span>
                  <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=28" title="Edit section: Model selection criteria">
                   <span>
                    edit
                   </span>
                  </a>
                  <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                   ]
                  </span>
                 </span>
                </div>
                <p>
                 <a href="/wiki/Model_selection" title="Model selection">
                  Model criteria selection
                 </a>
                 will select or model that more approximate true model. The
                 <a href="/wiki/Model_selection" title="Model selection">
                  Akaike's Information Criterion (AIC)
                 </a>
                 and The
                 <a href="/wiki/Model_selection" title="Model selection">
                  Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC)
                 </a>
                 are examples of asymptotically efficient criteria.
                </p>
                <div class="mw-heading mw-heading2">
                 <h2 id="Developments_and_big_data">
                  Developments and big data
                 </h2>
                 <span class="mw-editsection">
                  <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                   [
                  </span>
                  <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=29" title="Edit section: Developments and big data">
                   <span>
                    edit
                   </span>
                  </a>
                  <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                   ]
                  </span>
                 </span>
                </div>
                <style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1305433154">
                 .mw-parser-output .ambox{border:1px solid #a2a9b1;border-left:10px solid #36c;background-color:#fbfbfb;box-sizing:border-box}.mw-parser-output .ambox+link+.ambox,.mw-parser-output .ambox+link+style+.ambox,.mw-parser-output .ambox+link+link+.ambox,.mw-parser-output .ambox+.mw-empty-elt+link+.ambox,.mw-parser-output .ambox+.mw-empty-elt+link+style+.ambox,.mw-parser-output .ambox+.mw-empty-elt+link+link+.ambox{margin-top:-1px}html body.mediawiki .mw-parser-output .ambox.mbox-small-left{margin:4px 1em 4px 0;overflow:hidden;width:238px;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:88%;line-height:1.25em}.mw-parser-output .ambox-speedy{border-left:10px solid #b32424;background-color:#fee7e6}.mw-parser-output .ambox-delete{border-left:10px solid #b32424}.mw-parser-output .ambox-content{border-left:10px solid #f28500}.mw-parser-output .ambox-style{border-left:10px solid #fc3}.mw-parser-output .ambox-move{border-left:10px solid #9932cc}.mw-parser-output .ambox-protection{border-left:10px solid #a2a9b1}.mw-parser-output .ambox .mbox-text{border:none;padding:0.25em 0.5em;width:100%}.mw-parser-output .ambox .mbox-image{border:none;padding:2px 0 2px 0.5em;text-align:center}.mw-parser-output .ambox .mbox-imageright{border:none;padding:2px 0.5em 2px 0;text-align:center}.mw-parser-output .ambox .mbox-empty-cell{border:none;padding:0;width:1px}.mw-parser-output .ambox .mbox-image-div{width:52px}@media(min-width:720px){.mw-parser-output .ambox{margin:0 10%}}@media print{body.ns-0 .mw-parser-output .ambox{display:none!important}}
                </style>
                <table class="box-More_citations_needed_section plainlinks metadata ambox ambox-content ambox-Refimprove" role="presentation">
                 <tbody>
                  <tr>
                   <td class="mbox-image">
                    <div class="mbox-image-div">
                     <span typeof="mw:File">
                      <a class="mw-file-description" href="/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg">
                       <img alt="icon" class="mw-file-element" data-file-height="399" data-file-width="512" decoding="async" height="39" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/9/99/Question_book-new.svg/60px-Question_book-new.svg.png" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/9/99/Question_book-new.svg/120px-Question_book-new.svg.png 2x" width="50"/>
                      </a>
                     </span>
                    </div>
                   </td>
                   <td class="mbox-text">
                    <div class="mbox-text-span">
                     This section
                     <b>
                      needs additional citations for
                      <a href="/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability" title="Wikipedia:Verifiability">
                       verification
                      </a>
                     </b>
                     .
                     <span class="hide-when-compact">
                      Please help
                      <a href="/wiki/Special:EditPage/Biostatistics" title="Special:EditPage/Biostatistics">
                       improve this article
                      </a>
                      by
                      <a href="/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners" title="Help:Referencing for beginners">
                       adding citations to reliable sources
                      </a>
                      in this section. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed.
                     </span>
                     <span class="date-container">
                      <i>
                       (
                       <span class="date">
                        December 2016
                       </span>
                       )
                      </i>
                     </span>
                     <span class="hide-when-compact">
                      <i>
                       (
                       <small>
                        <a href="/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal" title="Help:Maintenance template removal">
                         Learn how and when to remove this message
                        </a>
                       </small>
                       )
                      </i>
                     </span>
                    </div>
                   </td>
                  </tr>
                 </tbody>
                </table>
                <p>
                 Recent developments have made a large impact on biostatistics. Two important changes have been the ability to collect data on a high-throughput scale, and the ability to perform much more complex analysis using computational techniques. This comes from the development in areas as
                 <a href="/wiki/DNA_sequencing" title="DNA sequencing">
                  sequencing
                 </a>
                 technologies,
                 <a href="/wiki/Bioinformatics" title="Bioinformatics">
                  Bioinformatics
                 </a>
                 and
                 <a href="/wiki/Machine_learning" title="Machine learning">
                  Machine learning
                 </a>
                 (
                 <a href="/wiki/Machine_learning_in_bioinformatics" title="Machine learning in bioinformatics">
                  Machine learning in bioinformatics
                 </a>
                 ).
                </p>
                <div class="mw-heading mw-heading3">
                 <h3 id="Use_in_high-throughput_data">
                  Use in high-throughput data
                 </h3>
                 <span class="mw-editsection">
                  <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                   [
                  </span>
                  <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=30" title="Edit section: Use in high-throughput data">
                   <span>
                    edit
                   </span>
                  </a>
                  <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                   ]
                  </span>
                 </span>
                </div>
                <p>
                 New biomedical technologies like
                 <a href="/wiki/DNA_microarray" title="DNA microarray">
                  microarrays
                 </a>
                 ,
                 <a href="/wiki/DNA_sequencing" title="DNA sequencing">
                  next-generation sequencers
                 </a>
                 (for genomics) and
                 <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Mass_spectrometers" title="Mass spectrometers">
                  mass spectrometry
                 </a>
                 (for proteomics) generate enormous amounts of data, allowing many tests to be performed simultaneously.
                 <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-20">
                  <a href="#cite_note-20">
                   <span class="cite-bracket">
                    [
                   </span>
                   20
                   <span class="cite-bracket">
                    ]
                   </span>
                  </a>
                 </sup>
                 Careful analysis with biostatistical methods is required to separate the signal from the noise. For example, a microarray could be used to measure many thousands of genes simultaneously, determining which of them have different expression in diseased cells compared to normal cells. However, only a fraction of genes will be differentially expressed.
                 <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-21">
                  <a href="#cite_note-21">
                   <span class="cite-bracket">
                    [
                   </span>
                   21
                   <span class="cite-bracket">
                    ]
                   </span>
                  </a>
                 </sup>
                </p>
                <p>
                 <a href="/wiki/Multicollinearity" title="Multicollinearity">
                  Multicollinearity
                 </a>
                 often occurs in high-throughput biostatistical settings. Due to high intercorrelation between the predictors (such as
                 <a href="/wiki/Gene_expression" title="Gene expression">
                  gene expression
                 </a>
                 levels), the information of one predictor might be contained in another one. It could be that only 5% of the predictors are responsible for 90% of the variability of the response. In such a case, one could apply the biostatistical technique of dimension reduction (for example via principal component analysis). Classical statistical techniques like linear or
                 <a href="/wiki/Logistic_regression" title="Logistic regression">
                  logistic regression
                 </a>
                 and
                 <a href="/wiki/Linear_discriminant_analysis" title="Linear discriminant analysis">
                  linear discriminant analysis
                 </a>
                 do not work well for high dimensional data (i.e. when the number of observations n is smaller than the number of features or predictors p: n &lt; p). As a matter of fact, one can get quite high R
                 <sup>
                  2
                 </sup>
                 -values despite very low predictive power of the statistical model. These classical statistical techniques (esp.
                 <a href="/wiki/Least_squares" title="Least squares">
                  least squares
                 </a>
                 linear regression) were developed for low dimensional data (i.e. where the number of observations n is much larger than the number of predictors p: n &gt;&gt; p). In cases of high dimensionality, one should always consider an independent validation test set and the corresponding residual sum of squares (RSS) and R
                 <sup>
                  2
                 </sup>
                 of the validation test set, not those of the training set.
                </p>
                <p>
                 Often, it is useful to pool information from multiple predictors together. For example,
                 <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Gene_Set_Enrichment_Analysis" title="Gene Set Enrichment Analysis">
                  Gene Set Enrichment Analysis
                 </a>
                 (GSEA) considers the perturbation of whole (functionally related) gene sets rather than of single genes.
                 <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-22">
                  <a href="#cite_note-22">
                   <span class="cite-bracket">
                    [
                   </span>
                   22
                   <span class="cite-bracket">
                    ]
                   </span>
                  </a>
                 </sup>
                 These gene sets might be known biochemical pathways or otherwise functionally related genes. The advantage of this approach is that it is more robust: It is more likely that a single gene is found to be falsely perturbed than it is that a whole pathway is falsely perturbed. Furthermore, one can integrate the accumulated knowledge about biochemical pathways (like the
                 <a href="/wiki/JAK-STAT_signaling_pathway" title="JAK-STAT signaling pathway">
                  JAK-STAT signaling pathway
                 </a>
                 ) using this approach.
                </p>
                <div class="mw-heading mw-heading3">
                 <h3 id="Bioinformatics_advances_in_databases,_data_mining,_and_biological_interpretation">
                  <span id="Bioinformatics_advances_in_databases.2C_data_mining.2C_and_biological_interpretation">
                  </span>
                  Bioinformatics advances in databases, data mining, and biological interpretation
                 </h3>
                 <span class="mw-editsection">
                  <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                   [
                  </span>
                  <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=31" title="Edit section: Bioinformatics advances in databases, data mining, and biological interpretation">
                   <span>
                    edit
                   </span>
                  </a>
                  <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                   ]
                  </span>
                 </span>
                </div>
                <p>
                 The development of
                 <a href="/wiki/Biological_database" title="Biological database">
                  biological databases
                 </a>
                 enables storage and management of biological data with the possibility of ensuring access for users around the world. They are useful for researchers depositing data, retrieve information and files (raw or processed) originated from other experiments or indexing scientific articles, as
                 <a href="/wiki/PubMed" title="PubMed">
                  PubMed
                 </a>
                 . Another possibility is search for the desired term (a gene, a protein, a disease, an organism, and so on) and check all results related to this search. There are databases dedicated to
                 <a href="/wiki/Single-nucleotide_polymorphism" title="Single-nucleotide polymorphism">
                  SNPs
                 </a>
                 (
                 <a href="/wiki/DbSNP" title="DbSNP">
                  dbSNP
                 </a>
                 ), the knowledge on genes characterization and their pathways (
                 <a href="/wiki/KEGG" title="KEGG">
                  KEGG
                 </a>
                 ) and the description of gene function classifying it by cellular component, molecular function and biological process (
                 <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Gene_ontology" title="Gene ontology">
                  Gene Ontology
                 </a>
                 ).
                 <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-:4_23-0">
                  <a href="#cite_note-:4-23">
                   <span class="cite-bracket">
                    [
                   </span>
                   23
                   <span class="cite-bracket">
                    ]
                   </span>
                  </a>
                 </sup>
                 In addition to databases that contain specific molecular information, there are others that are ample in the sense that they store information about an organism or group of organisms. As an example of a database directed towards just one organism, but that contains much data about it, is the
                 <i>
                  <a href="/wiki/Arabidopsis_thaliana" title="Arabidopsis thaliana">
                   Arabidopsis thaliana
                  </a>
                 </i>
                 genetic and molecular database – TAIR.
                 <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-24">
                  <a href="#cite_note-24">
                   <span class="cite-bracket">
                    [
                   </span>
                   24
                   <span class="cite-bracket">
                    ]
                   </span>
                  </a>
                 </sup>
                 Phytozome,
                 <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-25">
                  <a href="#cite_note-25">
                   <span class="cite-bracket">
                    [
                   </span>
                   25
                   <span class="cite-bracket">
                    ]
                   </span>
                  </a>
                 </sup>
                 in turn, stores the assemblies and annotation files of dozen of plant genomes, also containing visualization and analysis tools. Moreover, there is an interconnection between some databases in the information exchange/sharing and a major initiative was the
                 <a href="/wiki/International_Nucleotide_Sequence_Database_Collaboration" title="International Nucleotide Sequence Database Collaboration">
                  International Nucleotide Sequence Database Collaboration
                 </a>
                 (INSDC)
                 <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-26">
                  <a href="#cite_note-26">
                   <span class="cite-bracket">
                    [
                   </span>
                   26
                   <span class="cite-bracket">
                    ]
                   </span>
                  </a>
                 </sup>
                 which relates data from DDBJ,
                 <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-27">
                  <a href="#cite_note-27">
                   <span class="cite-bracket">
                    [
                   </span>
                   27
                   <span class="cite-bracket">
                    ]
                   </span>
                  </a>
                 </sup>
                 EMBL-EBI,
                 <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-28">
                  <a href="#cite_note-28">
                   <span class="cite-bracket">
                    [
                   </span>
                   28
                   <span class="cite-bracket">
                    ]
                   </span>
                  </a>
                 </sup>
                 and NCBI.
                 <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-29">
                  <a href="#cite_note-29">
                   <span class="cite-bracket">
                    [
                   </span>
                   29
                   <span class="cite-bracket">
                    ]
                   </span>
                  </a>
                 </sup>
                </p>
                <p>
                 Nowadays, increase in size and complexity of molecular datasets leads to use of powerful statistical methods provided by computer science algorithms which are developed by
                 <a href="/wiki/Machine_learning" title="Machine learning">
                  machine learning
                 </a>
                 area. Therefore, data mining and machine learning allow detection of patterns in data with a complex structure, as biological ones, by using methods of
                 <a href="/wiki/Supervised_learning" title="Supervised learning">
                  supervised
                 </a>
                 and
                 <a href="/wiki/Unsupervised_learning" title="Unsupervised learning">
                  unsupervised learning
                 </a>
                 , regression, detection of
                 <a href="/wiki/Cluster_analysis" title="Cluster analysis">
                  clusters
                 </a>
                 and
                 <a href="/wiki/Association_rule_learning" title="Association rule learning">
                  association rule mining
                 </a>
                 , among others.
                 <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-:4_23-1">
                  <a href="#cite_note-:4-23">
                   <span class="cite-bracket">
                    [
                   </span>
                   23
                   <span class="cite-bracket">
                    ]
                   </span>
                  </a>
                 </sup>
                 To indicate some of them,
                 <a href="/wiki/Self-organizing_map" title="Self-organizing map">
                  self-organizing maps
                 </a>
                 and
                 <a href="/wiki/K-means_clustering" title="K-means clustering">
                  <i>
                   k
                  </i>
                  -means
                 </a>
                 are examples of cluster algorithms;
                 <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Artificial_neural_network" title="Artificial neural network">
                  neural networks
                 </a>
                 implementation and
                 <a href="/wiki/Support_vector_machine" title="Support vector machine">
                  support vector machines
                 </a>
                 models are examples of common machine learning algorithms.
                </p>
                <p>
                 Collaborative work among molecular biologists, bioinformaticians, statisticians and computer scientists is important to perform an experiment correctly, going from planning, passing through data generation and analysis, and ending with biological interpretation of the results.
                 <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-:4_23-2">
                  <a href="#cite_note-:4-23">
                   <span class="cite-bracket">
                    [
                   </span>
                   23
                   <span class="cite-bracket">
                    ]
                   </span>
                  </a>
                 </sup>
                </p>
                <div class="mw-heading mw-heading3">
                 <h3 id="Use_of_computationally_intensive_methods">
                  Use of computationally intensive methods
                 </h3>
                 <span class="mw-editsection">
                  <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                   [
                  </span>
                  <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=32" title="Edit section: Use of computationally intensive methods">
                   <span>
                    edit
                   </span>
                  </a>
                  <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                   ]
                  </span>
                 </span>
                </div>
                <p>
                 On the other hand, the advent of modern computer technology and relatively cheap computing resources have enabled computer-intensive biostatistical methods like
                 <a href="/wiki/Bootstrapping_(statistics)" title="Bootstrapping (statistics)">
                  bootstrapping
                 </a>
                 and
                 <a class="new" href="/w/index.php?title=Re-sampling_(statistics)&amp;action=edit&amp;redlink=1" title="Re-sampling (statistics) (page does not exist)">
                  re-sampling
                 </a>
                 methods.
                </p>
                <p>
                 In recent times,
                 <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Random_forests" title="Random forests">
                  random forests
                 </a>
                 have gained popularity as a method for performing
                 <a href="/wiki/Statistical_classification" title="Statistical classification">
                  statistical classification
                 </a>
                 . Random forest techniques generate a panel of decision trees. Decision trees have the advantage that you can draw them and interpret them (even with a basic understanding of mathematics and statistics). Random Forests have thus been used for clinical decision support systems.
                 <sup class="noprint Inline-Template Template-Fact" style="white-space:nowrap;">
                  [
                  <i>
                   <a href="/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed" title="Wikipedia:Citation needed">
                    <span title="This claim needs references to reliable sources. (December 2016)">
                     citation needed
                    </span>
                   </a>
                  </i>
                  ]
                 </sup>
                </p>
                <div class="mw-heading mw-heading2">
                 <h2 id="Applications">
                  Applications
                 </h2>
                 <span class="mw-editsection">
                  <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                   [
                  </span>
                  <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=33" title="Edit section: Applications">
                   <span>
                    edit
                   </span>
                  </a>
                  <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                   ]
                  </span>
                 </span>
                </div>
                <link href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1305433154" rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style">
                 <table class="box-Prose plainlinks metadata ambox ambox-style ambox-Prose" role="presentation">
                  <tbody>
                   <tr>
                    <td class="mbox-image">
                     <div class="mbox-image-div">
                      <span typeof="mw:File">
                       <span>
                        <img alt="" class="mw-file-element" data-file-height="48" data-file-width="48" decoding="async" height="40" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/f/f2/Edit-clear.svg/40px-Edit-clear.svg.png" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/f/f2/Edit-clear.svg/120px-Edit-clear.svg.png 2x" width="40"/>
                       </span>
                      </span>
                     </div>
                    </td>
                    <td class="mbox-text">
                     <div class="mbox-text-span">
                      This section
                      <b>
                       is in
                       <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/MOS:LIST" title="MOS:LIST">
                        list
                       </a>
                       format but may read better as
                       <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/MOS:PROSE" title="MOS:PROSE">
                        prose
                       </a>
                      </b>
                      .
                      <span class="hide-when-compact">
                       You can help by
                       <a class="external text" href="https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit">
                        converting this section
                       </a>
                       , if appropriate.
                       <a href="/wiki/Help:Editing" title="Help:Editing">
                        Editing help
                       </a>
                       is available.
                      </span>
                      <span class="date-container">
                       <i>
                        (
                        <span class="date">
                         March 2016
                        </span>
                        )
                       </i>
                      </span>
                     </div>
                    </td>
                   </tr>
                  </tbody>
                 </table>
                 <div class="mw-heading mw-heading3">
                  <h3 id="Public_health">
                   Public health
                  </h3>
                  <span class="mw-editsection">
                   <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                    [
                   </span>
                   <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=34" title="Edit section: Public health">
                    <span>
                     edit
                    </span>
                   </a>
                   <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                    ]
                   </span>
                  </span>
                 </div>
                 <p>
                  <a href="/wiki/Public_health" title="Public health">
                   Public health
                  </a>
                  , including
                  <a href="/wiki/Epidemiology" title="Epidemiology">
                   epidemiology
                  </a>
                  ,
                  <a href="/wiki/Health_services_research" title="Health services research">
                   health services research
                  </a>
                  ,
                  <a href="/wiki/Nutrition" title="Nutrition">
                   nutrition
                  </a>
                  ,
                  <a href="/wiki/Environmental_health" title="Environmental health">
                   environmental health
                  </a>
                  and health care policy &amp; management. In these
                  <a href="/wiki/Medicine" title="Medicine">
                   medicine
                  </a>
                  contents, it's important to consider the design and analysis of the
                  <a href="/wiki/Clinical_trial" title="Clinical trial">
                   clinical trials
                  </a>
                  . As one example, there is the assessment of severity state of a patient with a prognosis of an outcome of a disease.
                 </p>
                 <p>
                  With new technologies and genetics knowledge, biostatistics are now also used for
                  <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Systems_medicine" title="Systems medicine">
                   Systems medicine
                  </a>
                  , which consists in a more personalized medicine. For this, is made an integration of data from different sources, including conventional patient data, clinico-pathological parameters, molecular and genetic data as well as data generated by additional new-omics technologies.
                  <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-30">
                   <a href="#cite_note-30">
                    <span class="cite-bracket">
                     [
                    </span>
                    30
                    <span class="cite-bracket">
                     ]
                    </span>
                   </a>
                  </sup>
                 </p>
                 <div class="mw-heading mw-heading3">
                  <h3 id="Quantitative_genetics">
                   Quantitative genetics
                  </h3>
                  <span class="mw-editsection">
                   <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                    [
                   </span>
                   <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=35" title="Edit section: Quantitative genetics">
                    <span>
                     edit
                    </span>
                   </a>
                   <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                    ]
                   </span>
                  </span>
                 </div>
                 <p>
                  The study of
                  <a href="/wiki/Population_genetics" title="Population genetics">
                   population genetics
                  </a>
                  and
                  <a href="/wiki/Statistical_genetics" title="Statistical genetics">
                   statistical genetics
                  </a>
                  in order to link variation in
                  <a href="/wiki/Genotype" title="Genotype">
                   genotype
                  </a>
                  with a variation in
                  <a href="/wiki/Phenotype" title="Phenotype">
                   phenotype
                  </a>
                  . In other words, it is desirable to discover the genetic basis of a measurable trait, a quantitative trait, that is under polygenic control. A genome region that is responsible for a continuous trait is called a
                  <a href="/wiki/Quantitative_trait_locus" title="Quantitative trait locus">
                   quantitative trait locus
                  </a>
                  (QTL). The study of QTLs become feasible by using
                  <a href="/wiki/Molecular_marker" title="Molecular marker">
                   molecular markers
                  </a>
                  and measuring traits in populations, but their mapping needs the obtaining of a population from an experimental crossing, like an F2 or
                  <a href="/wiki/Recombinant_inbred_strain" title="Recombinant inbred strain">
                   recombinant inbred strains
                  </a>
                  /lines (RILs). To scan for QTLs regions in a genome, a
                  <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Gene_map" title="Gene map">
                   gene map
                  </a>
                  based on linkage have to be built. Some of the best-known QTL mapping algorithms are Interval Mapping, Composite Interval Mapping, and Multiple Interval Mapping.
                  <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-31">
                   <a href="#cite_note-31">
                    <span class="cite-bracket">
                     [
                    </span>
                    31
                    <span class="cite-bracket">
                     ]
                    </span>
                   </a>
                  </sup>
                 </p>
                 <p>
                  However, QTL mapping resolution is impaired by the amount of recombination assayed, a problem for species in which it is difficult to obtain large offspring. Furthermore, allele diversity is restricted to individuals originated from contrasting parents, which limit studies of allele diversity when we have a panel of individuals representing a natural population.
                  <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-32">
                   <a href="#cite_note-32">
                    <span class="cite-bracket">
                     [
                    </span>
                    32
                    <span class="cite-bracket">
                     ]
                    </span>
                   </a>
                  </sup>
                  For this reason, the
                  <a href="/wiki/Genome-wide_association_study" title="Genome-wide association study">
                   genome-wide association study
                  </a>
                  was proposed in order to identify QTLs based on
                  <a href="/wiki/Linkage_disequilibrium" title="Linkage disequilibrium">
                   linkage disequilibrium
                  </a>
                  , that is the non-random association between traits and molecular markers. It was leveraged by the development of high-throughput
                  <a href="/wiki/SNP_genotyping" title="SNP genotyping">
                   SNP genotyping
                  </a>
                  .
                  <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-33">
                   <a href="#cite_note-33">
                    <span class="cite-bracket">
                     [
                    </span>
                    33
                    <span class="cite-bracket">
                     ]
                    </span>
                   </a>
                  </sup>
                 </p>
                 <p>
                  In
                  <a href="/wiki/Animal_breeding" title="Animal breeding">
                   animal
                  </a>
                  and
                  <a href="/wiki/Plant_breeding" title="Plant breeding">
                   plant breeding
                  </a>
                  , the use of markers in
                  <a href="/wiki/Selective_breeding" title="Selective breeding">
                   selection
                  </a>
                  aiming for breeding, mainly the molecular ones, collaborated to the development of
                  <a href="/wiki/Marker-assisted_selection" title="Marker-assisted selection">
                   marker-assisted selection
                  </a>
                  . While QTL mapping is limited due resolution, GWAS does not have enough power when rare variants of small effect that are also influenced by environment. So, the concept of Genomic Selection (GS) arises in order to use all molecular markers in the selection and allow the prediction of the performance of candidates in this selection. The proposal is to genotype and phenotype a training population, develop a model that can obtain the genomic estimated breeding values (GEBVs) of individuals belonging to a genotype and but not phenotype population, called testing population.
                  <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-34">
                   <a href="#cite_note-34">
                    <span class="cite-bracket">
                     [
                    </span>
                    34
                    <span class="cite-bracket">
                     ]
                    </span>
                   </a>
                  </sup>
                  This kind of study could also include a validation population, thinking in the concept of
                  <a href="/wiki/Cross-validation_(statistics)" title="Cross-validation (statistics)">
                   cross-validation
                  </a>
                  , in which the real phenotype results measured in this population are compared with the phenotype results based on the prediction, what used to check the accuracy of the model.
                 </p>
                 <p>
                  As a summary, some points about the application of quantitative genetics are:
                 </p>
                 <ul>
                  <li>
                   This has been used in agriculture to improve crops (
                   <a href="/wiki/Plant_breeding" title="Plant breeding">
                    Plant breeding
                   </a>
                   ) and
                   <a href="/wiki/Livestock" title="Livestock">
                    livestock
                   </a>
                   (
                   <a href="/wiki/Animal_breeding" title="Animal breeding">
                    Animal breeding
                   </a>
                   ).
                  </li>
                  <li>
                   In biomedical research, this work can assist in finding candidates
                   <a href="/wiki/Gene" title="Gene">
                    gene
                   </a>
                   <a href="/wiki/Allele" title="Allele">
                    alleles
                   </a>
                   that can cause or influence predisposition to diseases in
                   <a href="/wiki/Human_genetics" title="Human genetics">
                    human genetics
                   </a>
                  </li>
                 </ul>
                 <div class="mw-heading mw-heading3">
                  <h3 id="Expression_data">
                   Expression data
                  </h3>
                  <span class="mw-editsection">
                   <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                    [
                   </span>
                   <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=36" title="Edit section: Expression data">
                    <span>
                     edit
                    </span>
                   </a>
                   <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                    ]
                   </span>
                  </span>
                 </div>
                 <p>
                  Studies for differential expression of genes from
                  <a href="/wiki/RNA-Seq" title="RNA-Seq">
                   RNA-Seq
                  </a>
                  data, as for
                  <a href="/wiki/Real-time_polymerase_chain_reaction" title="Real-time polymerase chain reaction">
                   RT-qPCR
                  </a>
                  and
                  <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Microarrays" title="Microarrays">
                   microarrays
                  </a>
                  , demands comparison of conditions. The goal is to identify genes which have a significant change in abundance between different conditions. Then, experiments are designed appropriately, with replicates for each condition/treatment, randomization and blocking, when necessary. In RNA-Seq, the quantification of expression uses the information of mapped reads that are summarized in some genetic unit, as
                  <a href="/wiki/Exon" title="Exon">
                   exons
                  </a>
                  that are part of a gene sequence. As
                  <a href="/wiki/Microarray" title="Microarray">
                   microarray
                  </a>
                  results can be approximated by a normal distribution, RNA-Seq counts data are better explained by other distributions. The first used distribution was the
                  <a href="/wiki/Poisson_distribution" title="Poisson distribution">
                   Poisson
                  </a>
                  one, but it underestimate the sample error, leading to false positives. Currently, biological variation is considered by methods that estimate a dispersion parameter of a
                  <a href="/wiki/Negative_binomial_distribution" title="Negative binomial distribution">
                   negative binomial distribution
                  </a>
                  .
                  <a href="/wiki/Generalized_linear_model" title="Generalized linear model">
                   Generalized linear models
                  </a>
                  are used to perform the tests for statistical significance and as the number of genes is high, multiple tests correction have to be considered.
                  <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-35">
                   <a href="#cite_note-35">
                    <span class="cite-bracket">
                     [
                    </span>
                    35
                    <span class="cite-bracket">
                     ]
                    </span>
                   </a>
                  </sup>
                  Some examples of other analysis on
                  <a href="/wiki/Genomics" title="Genomics">
                   genomics
                  </a>
                  data comes from microarray or
                  <a href="/wiki/Proteomics" title="Proteomics">
                   proteomics
                  </a>
                  experiments.
                  <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-36">
                   <a href="#cite_note-36">
                    <span class="cite-bracket">
                     [
                    </span>
                    36
                    <span class="cite-bracket">
                     ]
                    </span>
                   </a>
                  </sup>
                  <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-37">
                   <a href="#cite_note-37">
                    <span class="cite-bracket">
                     [
                    </span>
                    37
                    <span class="cite-bracket">
                     ]
                    </span>
                   </a>
                  </sup>
                  Often concerning diseases or disease stages.
                  <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-38">
                   <a href="#cite_note-38">
                    <span class="cite-bracket">
                     [
                    </span>
                    38
                    <span class="cite-bracket">
                     ]
                    </span>
                   </a>
                  </sup>
                 </p>
                 <div class="mw-heading mw-heading3">
                  <h3 id="Other_studies">
                   Other studies
                  </h3>
                  <span class="mw-editsection">
                   <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                    [
                   </span>
                   <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=37" title="Edit section: Other studies">
                    <span>
                     edit
                    </span>
                   </a>
                   <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                    ]
                   </span>
                  </span>
                 </div>
                 <ul>
                  <li>
                   <a href="/wiki/Ecology" title="Ecology">
                    Ecology
                   </a>
                   ,
                   <a href="/wiki/Ecological_forecasting" title="Ecological forecasting">
                    ecological forecasting
                   </a>
                  </li>
                  <li>
                   Biological
                   <a href="/wiki/Sequence_analysis" title="Sequence analysis">
                    sequence analysis
                   </a>
                   <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-39">
                    <a href="#cite_note-39">
                     <span class="cite-bracket">
                      [
                     </span>
                     39
                     <span class="cite-bracket">
                      ]
                     </span>
                    </a>
                   </sup>
                  </li>
                  <li>
                   <a href="/wiki/Systems_biology" title="Systems biology">
                    Systems biology
                   </a>
                   for gene network inference or pathways analysis.
                   <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-40">
                    <a href="#cite_note-40">
                     <span class="cite-bracket">
                      [
                     </span>
                     40
                     <span class="cite-bracket">
                      ]
                     </span>
                    </a>
                   </sup>
                  </li>
                  <li>
                   <a href="/wiki/Clinical_research" title="Clinical research">
                    Clinical research
                   </a>
                   and pharmaceutical development
                  </li>
                  <li>
                   <a href="/wiki/Population_dynamics" title="Population dynamics">
                    Population dynamics
                   </a>
                   , especially in regards to
                   <a href="/wiki/Fisheries_science" title="Fisheries science">
                    fisheries science
                   </a>
                   .
                  </li>
                  <li>
                   <a href="/wiki/Phylogenetics" title="Phylogenetics">
                    Phylogenetics
                   </a>
                   and
                   <a href="/wiki/Evolution" title="Evolution">
                    evolution
                   </a>
                  </li>
                  <li>
                   <a href="/wiki/Pharmacodynamics" title="Pharmacodynamics">
                    Pharmacodynamics
                   </a>
                  </li>
                  <li>
                   <a href="/wiki/Pharmacokinetics" title="Pharmacokinetics">
                    Pharmacokinetics
                   </a>
                  </li>
                  <li>
                   <a href="/wiki/Neuroimaging" title="Neuroimaging">
                    Neuroimaging
                   </a>
                  </li>
                 </ul>
                 <div class="mw-heading mw-heading2">
                  <h2 id="Tools">
                   Tools
                  </h2>
                  <span class="mw-editsection">
                   <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                    [
                   </span>
                   <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=38" title="Edit section: Tools">
                    <span>
                     edit
                    </span>
                   </a>
                   <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                    ]
                   </span>
                  </span>
                 </div>
                 <p>
                  There are a lot of tools that can be used to do statistical analysis in biological data. Most of them are useful in other areas of knowledge, covering a large number of applications (alphabetical). Here are brief descriptions of some of them:
                 </p>
                 <ul>
                  <li>
                   <a href="/wiki/ASReml" title="ASReml">
                    ASReml
                   </a>
                   : Another software developed by VSNi
                   <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-vsni_41-0">
                    <a href="#cite_note-vsni-41">
                     <span class="cite-bracket">
                      [
                     </span>
                     41
                     <span class="cite-bracket">
                      ]
                     </span>
                    </a>
                   </sup>
                   that can be used also in R environment as a package. It is developed to estimate variance components under a general linear mixed model using
                   <a href="/wiki/Restricted_maximum_likelihood" title="Restricted maximum likelihood">
                    restricted maximum likelihood
                   </a>
                   (REML). Models with fixed effects and random effects and nested or crossed ones are allowed. Gives the possibility to investigate different
                   <a href="/wiki/Covariance_matrix" title="Covariance matrix">
                    variance-covariance
                   </a>
                   matrix structures.
                  </li>
                  <li>
                   CycDesigN:
                   <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-42">
                    <a href="#cite_note-42">
                     <span class="cite-bracket">
                      [
                     </span>
                     42
                     <span class="cite-bracket">
                      ]
                     </span>
                    </a>
                   </sup>
                   A computer package developed by VSNi
                   <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-vsni_41-1">
                    <a href="#cite_note-vsni-41">
                     <span class="cite-bracket">
                      [
                     </span>
                     41
                     <span class="cite-bracket">
                      ]
                     </span>
                    </a>
                   </sup>
                   that helps the researchers create experimental designs and analyze data coming from a design present in one of three classes handled by CycDesigN. These classes are resolvable, non-resolvable, partially replicated and
                   <a href="/wiki/Crossover_study" title="Crossover study">
                    crossover designs
                   </a>
                   . It includes less used designs the Latinized ones, as t-Latinized design.
                   <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-43">
                    <a href="#cite_note-43">
                     <span class="cite-bracket">
                      [
                     </span>
                     43
                     <span class="cite-bracket">
                      ]
                     </span>
                    </a>
                   </sup>
                  </li>
                  <li>
                   <a href="/wiki/Orange_(software)" title="Orange (software)">
                    Orange
                   </a>
                   : A programming interface for high-level data processing, data mining and data visualization. Include tools for gene expression and genomics.
                   <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-:4_23-3">
                    <a href="#cite_note-:4-23">
                     <span class="cite-bracket">
                      [
                     </span>
                     23
                     <span class="cite-bracket">
                      ]
                     </span>
                    </a>
                   </sup>
                  </li>
                  <li>
                   <a href="/wiki/R_(programming_language)" title="R (programming language)">
                    R
                   </a>
                   : An
                   <a href="/wiki/Open_source" title="Open source">
                    open source
                   </a>
                   environment and programming language dedicated to statistical computing and graphics. It is an implementation of
                   <a href="/wiki/S_(programming_language)" title="S (programming language)">
                    S
                   </a>
                   language maintained by CRAN.
                   <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-44">
                    <a href="#cite_note-44">
                     <span class="cite-bracket">
                      [
                     </span>
                     44
                     <span class="cite-bracket">
                      ]
                     </span>
                    </a>
                   </sup>
                   In addition to its functions to read data tables, take descriptive statistics, develop and evaluate models, its repository contains packages developed by researchers around the world. This allows the development of functions written to deal with the statistical analysis of data that comes from specific applications.
                   <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-45">
                    <a href="#cite_note-45">
                     <span class="cite-bracket">
                      [
                     </span>
                     45
                     <span class="cite-bracket">
                      ]
                     </span>
                    </a>
                   </sup>
                   In the case of Bioinformatics, for example, there are packages located in the main repository (CRAN) and in others, as
                   <a href="/wiki/Bioconductor" title="Bioconductor">
                    Bioconductor
                   </a>
                   . It is also possible to use packages under development that are shared in hosting-services as
                   <a href="/wiki/GitHub" title="GitHub">
                    GitHub
                   </a>
                   .
                  </li>
                  <li>
                   <a href="/wiki/SAS_(software)" title="SAS (software)">
                    SAS
                   </a>
                   : A data analysis software widely used, going through universities, services and industry. Developed by a company with the same name (
                   <a href="/wiki/SAS_Institute" title="SAS Institute">
                    SAS Institute
                   </a>
                   ), it uses
                   <a href="/wiki/SAS_language" title="SAS language">
                    SAS language
                   </a>
                   for programming.
                  </li>
                  <li>
                   PLA 3.0:
                   <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-46">
                    <a href="#cite_note-46">
                     <span class="cite-bracket">
                      [
                     </span>
                     46
                     <span class="cite-bracket">
                      ]
                     </span>
                    </a>
                   </sup>
                   Is a biostatistical analysis software for regulated environments (e.g. drug testing) which supports Quantitative Response Assays (Parallel-Line, Parallel-Logistics, Slope-Ratio) and Dichotomous Assays (Quantal Response, Binary Assays). It also supports weighting methods for combination calculations and the automatic data aggregation of independent assay data.
                  </li>
                  <li>
                   <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Weka_(machine_learning)" title="Weka (machine learning)">
                    Weka
                   </a>
                   : A
                   <a href="/wiki/Java_(programming_language)" title="Java (programming language)">
                    Java
                   </a>
                   software for
                   <a href="/wiki/Machine_learning" title="Machine learning">
                    machine learning
                   </a>
                   and
                   <a href="/wiki/Data_mining" title="Data mining">
                    data mining
                   </a>
                   , including tools and methods for visualization, clustering, regression, association rule, and classification. There are tools for cross-validation, bootstrapping and a module of algorithm comparison. Weka also can be run in other programming languages as Perl or R.
                   <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-:4_23-4">
                    <a href="#cite_note-:4-23">
                     <span class="cite-bracket">
                      [
                     </span>
                     23
                     <span class="cite-bracket">
                      ]
                     </span>
                    </a>
                   </sup>
                  </li>
                  <li>
                   <a href="/wiki/Python_(programming_language)" title="Python (programming language)">
                    Python (programming language)
                   </a>
                   image analysis, deep-learning, machine-learning
                  </li>
                  <li>
                   <a href="/wiki/SQL" title="SQL">
                    SQL
                   </a>
                   databases
                  </li>
                  <li>
                   <a href="/wiki/NoSQL" title="NoSQL">
                    NoSQL
                   </a>
                  </li>
                  <li>
                   <a href="/wiki/NumPy" title="NumPy">
                    NumPy
                   </a>
                   numerical python
                  </li>
                  <li>
                   <a href="/wiki/SciPy" title="SciPy">
                    SciPy
                   </a>
                  </li>
                  <li>
                   <a href="/wiki/SageMath" title="SageMath">
                    SageMath
                   </a>
                  </li>
                  <li>
                   <a href="/wiki/LAPACK" title="LAPACK">
                    LAPACK
                   </a>
                   linear algebra
                  </li>
                  <li>
                   <a href="/wiki/MATLAB" title="MATLAB">
                    MATLAB
                   </a>
                  </li>
                  <li>
                   <a href="/wiki/Apache_Hadoop" title="Apache Hadoop">
                    Apache Hadoop
                   </a>
                  </li>
                  <li>
                   <a href="/wiki/Apache_Spark" title="Apache Spark">
                    Apache Spark
                   </a>
                  </li>
                  <li>
                   <a href="/wiki/Amazon_Web_Services" title="Amazon Web Services">
                    Amazon Web Services
                   </a>
                  </li>
                 </ul>
                 <div class="mw-heading mw-heading2">
                  <h2 id="Scope_and_training_programs">
                   Scope and training programs
                  </h2>
                  <span class="mw-editsection">
                   <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                    [
                   </span>
                   <a href="/w/index.php?title=Biostatistics&amp;action=edit&amp;section=39" title="Edit section: Scope and training programs">
                    <span>
                     edit
                    </span>
                   </a>
                   <span class="mw-editsection-bracket">
                    ]
                   </span>
                  </span>
                 </div>
                 <p>
                  Almost all educational programmes in biostatistics are at
                  <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Postgraduate" title="Postgraduate">
                   postgraduate
                  </a>
                  level. They are most often found in schools of public health, affiliated with schools of medicine, forestry, or agriculture, or as a focus of application in departments of statistics.
                 </p>
                 <p>
